author: Russell A. Barkley
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Behavior Regulation, Attention
briefOverview: Places visual cues and reminders at the exact location and moment where desired behavior is expected, externalizing rules for students who cannot self-regulate internally.
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) model of behavioral inhibition; Pfiffner et al. (2006, School Psychology Review); ADHD as performance deficit not knowledge deficit.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Identify point of performance (10 min). 2) Design physical cue (10 min). 3) Place at exact location. 4) Pair with reinforcer. 5) Fade systematically.
resourcesNeeded: Laminated reminder cards, colored stickers, desk-taped checklists, dry-erase markers. Classroom teacher implements; behavior specialist designs.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Stigma: use universal cues. Cues lose effect: change format/color. Teacher forgets: build into lesson planning.
caseStudySummary: Marcus, 4th grader, began math without reading directions. Orange STOP card on worksheets + point system. Within 2 weeks, reading directions 80% of assignments.
successCriteriaSummary: Target behavior 80%+ over 2 consecutive weeks; 50% reduction in verbal prompts; daily tracking sheet.

author: Russell A. Barkley
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Behavior Regulation, Attention
briefOverview: Provides immediate tangible reinforcement (tokens/points) for specific desired behaviors, exchangeable for preferred activities. Externalizes motivation that ADHD students cannot generate internally.
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) motivational deficit model; Fabiano et al. (2009, Clinical Psychology Review) meta-analysis: token economies are well-established treatments.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select 3-5 target behaviors. 2) Create point menu with student. 3) Build tracking tool. 4) Deliver points immediately. 5) Add response-cost after 1 week (3:1 ratio). 6) Allow exchanges daily. 7) Review biweekly.
resourcesNeeded: Point chart or ClassDojo, reward menu poster, tokens for younger students. Never take away more points than student has.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Other students want it: go classwide. Interest fades: rotate rewards every 2-3 weeks. Tracking burdensome: limit to 3 behaviors.
caseStudySummary: Aaliyah, 2nd grader ADHD-Combined, hand-raising increased 20%→75%, work completion 40%→70% within 3 weeks using clothespin-on-strip tracker.
successCriteriaSummary: Target behaviors 80%+ for 3 weeks; 50% reduction in ODRs; daily point card graphed weekly.

author: Russell A. Barkley
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Behavior Regulation, Attention
briefOverview: Structured daily communication tool: teacher rates 3-5 target behaviors, sends card home, parents deliver pre-arranged reward. Bridges school-home behavioral support.
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020); Volpe & Fabiano (2013) DRC evidence summary; What Works Clearinghouse strong evidence rating.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities; Anxiety; Depression
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Parent-teacher planning meeting (30 min). 2) Establish home rewards. 3) Create DRC form. 4) Rate daily (3-5 min). 5) Parents review/deliver consequences. 6) Monitor trends weekly.
resourcesNeeded: Printed DRC forms, take-home folder, digital option (email/ClassDojo). Start with low success criterion.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Card lost/hidden: send digital duplicate. Parent follow-through: simplify to one reward. Inconsistent rating: define levels concretely.
caseStudySummary: Jayden, 5th grader ADHD-Inattentive, homework completion rose from 40% to 85% over 4 weeks. Daily communication revealed transition-day struggles.
successCriteriaSummary: Meets criteria 80%+ of days over 4 weeks; measurable target behavior improvement; improved parent-teacher communication.

author: Russell A. Barkley
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Organization, Attention
briefOverview: Uses visual timers and countdown displays to make time passage concrete and visible. Addresses 'time blindness' — a core ADHD feature where future deadlines feel abstract.
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) time blindness model; Toplak et al. (2006, JCPP); Burkett et al. (2015) visual timer studies.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess time awareness (10 min). 2) Introduce visual timer (15 min). 3) Segment tasks into timed chunks. 4) Use time-anchored prompts. 5) Teach self-monitoring with time log. 6) Apply to transitions.
resourcesNeeded: Time Timer (~$30) or free apps, sand timers, time estimation recording sheet. Place at student eye level.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Anxiety about timer: reframe as helper; give extra time initially. Disrupts others: vibrating/visual-only timer. Student ignores: pair with token economy.
caseStudySummary: Sofia, 3rd grader, 45-min assignments became 15-min with Time Timer + Time Detective log. Estimates accurate within 3-5 min by week 3. Completion 50%→85%.
successCriteriaSummary: Completes timed assignments 75%+; time estimates within 20% accuracy in 4-6 weeks; 50% fewer teacher prompts.

author: Russell A. Barkley
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Organization, Academic Skills
briefOverview: External tools store information the student would otherwise need to hold in mind. Written directions, reference cards, checklists, and personal notepads replace limited internal working memory.
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) working memory as core EF deficit; Rapport et al. (2008, JACP); Martinussen et al. (2005, JEP).
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities; Speech/Language Impairment; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Audit working memory demands (20 min). 2) Provide written versions of oral directions. 3) Create task-specific reference cards. 4) Implement personal info pad. 5) Limit oral directions to 2 steps. 6) Use routine checklists.
resourcesNeeded: Personal whiteboard, laminated reference cards, sticky notes, checklist strips. Do NOT require copying from board.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Student doesn't use tools: explicitly teach + pair with tokens. Desk clutter: small caddy, limit 2-3 items. 'Crutch' concern: Barkley's glasses analogy.
caseStudySummary: Elijah, 6th grader ADHD+LD, paragraph scores improved 2/6→4/6 with whiteboard + writing card + direction slips. 60% fewer re-explanations.
successCriteriaSummary: Independent tool use 80%+ after 2-week teaching; accuracy improves 20+ pts; 50% fewer direction repetitions.

bookTitle: The Complete Guide to Asperger's Syndrome
author: Tony Attwood
functionalNeedAreas: Social Communication, Emotional Regulation
strategyName: Social Stories and Comic Strip Conversations
briefOverview: Short individualized narratives describing social situations with expected responses. Comic Strip Conversations use stick-figure drawings with speech/thought bubbles to map hidden conversation elements.
evidenceBaseSummary: Gray (1991) Social Stories; Attwood (2007) Ch. 5; Sansosti et al. (2004, Focus on Autism) meta-analysis; Gray & Garand (1993) sentence ratio guidelines.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language Impairment
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Identify target situation (10 min). 2) Comic Strip Conversation to gather perspectives (10 min). 3) Write Social Story following Gray's formula (20 min). 4) Read daily with student (5 min). 5) Review/revise weekly.
resourcesNeeded: Paper and colored markers, Carol Gray's New Social Story Book (2015), quiet private space. Digital: Pictello or Book Creator apps.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Patronizing for older students: frame as 'social briefing.' Doesn't generalize: create variations and debrief real incidents. Limited time: audio version.
caseStudySummary: Marcus, 4th grader ASD, walked away mid-conversation. Comic Strip revealed classmate felt ignored. Social Story taught exit phrase. After 3 weeks, used phrase 60% of time.
successCriteriaSummary: Target behavior in 4/5 opportunities within 4-6 weeks; student self-report on 1-5 ease scale; peer observation of improved inclusion.

bookTitle: The Complete Guide to Asperger's Syndrome
author: Tony Attwood
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation, Anxiety Management
briefOverview: Personalized set of calming strategies and cognitive restructuring techniques organized by emotional intensity level (1-5 thermometer). Student selects appropriate tool before reaching crisis.
evidenceBaseSummary: Attwood (2007) Ch. 6; Sofronoff, Attwood & Hinton (2005, JCPP) — CBT significantly reduced anxiety in Asperger's vs. waitlist control.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; Anxiety; Depression; ADHD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce emotional thermometer 1-5 (20 min). 2) Explore tool categories: physical, relaxation, social, thinking, special interest (2x20 min). 3) Build toolbox visual (15 min). 4) Practice in calm moments (5 min, 3x/week). 5) Prompt via private cue. 6) Debrief after incidents.
resourcesNeeded: Emotional thermometer visual, index cards or box, timer, cool-down space. School counselor for cognitive restructuring. Use special interest as metaphor.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Can't identify level: teach body-based cues first. Refuses toolbox: normalize classwide or make digital/private. Jumps 1→5: FBA for triggers + proactive use.
caseStudySummary: Priya, 6th grader ASD+anxiety, math test shutdowns. Toolbox: wall push-ups, 4-7-8 breathing, thinking card. Shutdowns decreased from 3/week to <1/month over 8 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: Reduced escalation frequency/intensity/duration; 80% accurate thermometer identification; independent tool use in 3/5 escalations.

bookTitle: The Complete Guide to Asperger's Syndrome
author: Tony Attwood
functionalNeedAreas: Academic Skills, Engagement, Motivation
briefOverview: Deliberately incorporates the student's intense special interests into academic instruction as a bridge to engagement, comprehension, and motivation. Treats interests as educational tools rather than distractions.
evidenceBaseSummary: Attwood (2007) Ch. 7; Winter-Messiers (2007, Focus on Autism) — increased motivation and writing quality; Mancil & Pearl (2008) — increased on-task behavior.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; ADHD; Specific Learning Disabilities
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Identify special interest via student/family interview (15 min). 2) Map to curriculum standards (20 min). 3) Embed in assignments with modified prompts (ongoing). 4) Use as structured reward with timer (ongoing). 5) Create peer connection opportunities.
resourcesNeeded: Student interest inventory, flexible assignment rubrics, timer. NEVER remove interest access as punishment — eliminates primary coping mechanism.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Only wants interest: visual schedule with first-then structure. Interest too narrow: think creatively (elevators→engineering, physics, math). Peers tease: frame as expertise.
caseStudySummary: Devon, 3rd grader ASD, interested in weather. Restructured reading to meteorology nonfiction; wrote storm chaser narrative; presented as class meteorologist. Fluency +15 WCPM; refusal daily→once/week.
successCriteriaSummary: Increased task completion on embedded assignments; reduced task refusal (frequency count); improved work quality (rubric); student engagement self-report.

bookTitle: The Complete Guide to Asperger's Syndrome
author: Tony Attwood
functionalNeedAreas: Social Skills, Communication, Pragmatic Language
briefOverview: Explicitly teaches social rules, conversational scripts, and interpersonal expectations through direct instruction, modeling, video analysis, role-play, and guided real-world application. Provides the 'operating manual' for social interaction.
evidenceBaseSummary: Attwood (2007) Ch. 3 & 5; Bellini (2006) Building Social Relationships; White et al. (2007, Clinical Psych Review) — moderate-to-large effect sizes for structured programs.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; Speech/Language Impairment; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess social skill gaps (30 min). 2) Teach rule explicitly with if/then algorithm (10-15 min). 3) Model correct/incorrect versions (10 min). 4) Role-play rehearsal (10-15 min, 2-3x/week). 5) Generalize to one daily natural situation. 6) Optional video self-modeling.
resourcesNeeded: Social rule cards, video clips, trained peer buddy, Social Thinking materials, private rehearsal space. Teach student as 'social scientist.'
challengesSolutionsSummary: Doesn't generalize: use cue cards in environment + peer buddy. Embarrassed by role-play: written scripts, puppets, digital avatars. Unpredictable situations: teach default response.
caseStudySummary: Aiden, 7th grader ASD, learned 3-step lunch approach: observe group, ask to sit, ask about topic. After 2 weeks role-play + daily coaching, successfully joined conversation. By week 6, peers began inviting him.
successCriteriaSummary: Target skill independently in 4/5 opportunities within 6-8 weeks; reduced social errors; improved peer acceptance; student confidence rating.

bookTitle: The Complete Guide to Asperger's Syndrome
author: Tony Attwood
functionalNeedAreas: Sensory Processing, Attention, Self-Regulation
briefOverview: Systematically identifies specific sensory sensitivities and modifies the classroom environment to reduce overload. Creates individualized Trigger→Accommodation map across auditory, visual, tactile, olfactory, and vestibular modalities.
evidenceBaseSummary: Attwood (2007) Ch. 11; Dunn (1999) Sensory Profile; Ben-Sasson et al. (2009, JADD) meta-analysis; DSM-5 sensory criteria for ASD.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; ADHD; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Sensory audit with OT (30-45 min). 2) Create Trigger→Accommodation map (20 min). 3) Modify physical environment. 4) Teach self-advocacy language. 5) Develop proactive sensory schedule. 6) Review quarterly.
resourcesNeeded: OT consultation (essential), noise-reducing headphones, fidgets, weighted lap pad, tinted overlays, alternative seating, retreat space, visual timer. Sensory passport for older students.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Others question accommodations: universal design framing. Over-uses breaks: ABC data + clear parameters. Fire drills: advance warning + headphones + buddy protocol. Subs unaware: one-page Sensory Quick Guide.
caseStudySummary: Lina, 2nd grader ASD, covered ears/crawled under desk. OT found fluorescent hum + announcement sensitivity. Window seat + headphones + warnings + movement break. Under-desk episodes daily→1 total; on-task 40%→85%.
successCriteriaSummary: Reduced sensory distress behaviors; increased on-task time; student identifies 3+ triggers and accommodations; independent self-advocacy in 4/5 situations.

author: Leah Kuypers
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation, Self-Regulation, Self-Awareness
briefOverview: Students categorize internal states into four color-coded zones (Blue=low, Green=ready, Yellow=heightened, Red=extreme). Structured check-ins at predictable intervals build emotional vocabulary and self-monitoring without judgment.
evidenceBaseSummary: Kuypers (2011); CBT principles; Alert Program (Williams & Shellenberger, 1996); Social Thinking (Winner, 2007); Durlak et al. (2011) SEL meta-analysis; 2019 JOTSEI pilot study.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Intellectual Disability; Specific Learning Disabilities
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce four zones with poster (20-30 min). 2) Model personal zone ID throughout day. 3) Daily check-in at morning + after lunch (2-3 min each). 4) Students share body clues (1-2 min). 5) Track patterns weekly.
resourcesNeeded: Zones poster, check-in cards/clip charts/digital tools, visual supports for nonverbal students. Start with two zones for younger/cognitively impacted students.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Always picks Green: normalize all zones by modeling Yellow/Blue; use private check-ins. Confuses zones: pair with body-sensation anchors. No time: embed in existing transitions.
caseStudySummary: Marcus, 4th grader ADHD, disrupted after recess. After Zones instruction, began moving clip to Yellow and independently taking walk-and-drink break. Post-recess redirections reduced 40% in one month.
successCriteriaSummary: Independent accurate zone ID 80%+ within 6 weeks; 25% reduction in behavioral incidents; names one body clue per zone.

author: Leah Kuypers
functionalNeedAreas: Self-Regulation, Sensory Processing, Impulse Control
briefOverview: Each student builds a personalized toolbox of calming, alerting, and focusing strategies (sensory, cognitive, movement, social) to move toward or stay in the Green Zone. Students trial tools and rate effectiveness personally.
evidenceBaseSummary: Sensory processing theory (Dunn, 1997); Alert Program (Williams & Shellenberger, 1996); Shanker (2012); Robson et al. (2020, Review of Education) meta-analysis — calming strategies improved behavior for ADHD/ASD.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Anxiety; Depression
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce toolbox concept (15-20 min). 2) Present categories with demos (20 min). 3) Students trial 2-3 tools per category over 1-2 weeks. 4) Create personal toolbox visual (30 min). 5) Prompt during dysregulation. 6) Update monthly.
resourcesNeeded: Toolbox template (laminated), fidgets, resistance bands, gum, stress balls, visual timer. Let students decorate for ownership. Picture menus for nonverbal students.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Over-relies on one tool: 'try something new' weekly goal. Tools become toys: teach expected vs. unexpected use. Others want items: make universal class activity.
caseStudySummary: Priya, 2nd grader anxiety, froze during writing. Toolbox: deep breathing, stress ball (3 squeezes), positive self-talk card. Teacher tapped toolbox card as cue. Independently initiated writing 7/10 sessions within 3 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: Names 3+ tools without prompting; independent use 2+/day within 4 weeks; 30% reduction in Yellow/Red Zone time over 6 weeks.

author: Leah Kuypers
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation, Flexible Thinking, Social Problem-Solving
briefOverview: Students categorize problems as small/medium/big and match reaction size to problem size. Teaches proportional emotional responding and prevents over-reacting to everyday situations.
evidenceBaseSummary: Social Thinking (Winner, 2007); Kuypers (2011); CBT cognitive reappraisal (Gross, 2002); Lochman et al. (2010) Coping Power Program; consistent with Tier 2 PBIS.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; ADHD; Anxiety; Specific Learning Disabilities; Speech/Language Impairment
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce size scale with visual chart (20 min). 2) Sort scenario cards as class (15 min, 2-3x first 2 weeks). 3) Teach matched reactions (15 min). 4) Real-time coaching during conflicts (1-2 min). 5) Weekly debrief (5-10 min).
resourcesNeeded: Size of Problem poster, scenario cards, desk strip for student reference. Use TV clips/book characters for low-stakes practice. Avoid using during active crisis.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Every problem is 'big': anchor question 'Will this matter tomorrow?' Feels invalidated: separate feelings from reactions. Doesn't generalize: share language with all staff.
caseStudySummary: Jamal, 3rd grader ASD, screamed at handwriting errors. Identified mistakes as 'small problems.' Desk-strip visual. Screaming 4/week→<1/week within 4 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: Accurately categorizes 4/5 scenarios within 4 weeks; 50% reduction in disproportionate reactions within 6 weeks; verbalizes expected reaction size.

author: Leah Kuypers
functionalNeedAreas: Social Awareness, Behavioral Self-Monitoring, Perspective-Taking
briefOverview: Students distinguish 'expected' (context-fitting) from 'unexpected' (confusing/disruptive) behaviors using neutral language instead of good/bad labels. Builds social awareness and self-monitoring by connecting actions to others' thoughts and feelings.
evidenceBaseSummary: Social Thinking (Winner, 2007); Kuypers (2011); Theory of Mind (Baron-Cohen, 1995); Crooke et al. (2008, JADD) — improved social responsiveness scores in ASD.
applicableDiagnoses: Autism Spectrum Disorder; ADHD; Speech/Language Impairment; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce vocabulary with T-chart (20 min). 2) Connect to zones (10 min). 3) Scenario analysis practice (15 min, 2-3x/week for 3 weeks). 4) Create setting-specific maps. 5) Cue in real time neutrally. 6) Reinforce with positive narration.
resourcesNeeded: Expected/Unexpected T-chart poster and student copies, scenario cards/video clips. Tie to specific contexts — expectations differ by setting.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Uses as insult: teach it's self-reflection only. Can't perspective-take: use thought bubbles and feeling faces. Rigid rule application: teach expectations change with place/people/time.
caseStudySummary: Sofia, 5th grader ASD+pragmatic language, made loud personal comments. Behavior map showed 'commenting on appearance' = unexpected → others feel uncomfortable. Comments decreased daily→~once/week; began self-correcting.
successCriteriaSummary: Correctly identifies expected/unexpected in 4/5 scenarios within 4 weeks; 40% reduction in target unexpected behaviors; 1+ self-monitoring instances per week.

author: Leah Kuypers
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation, Sensory Processing, Impulse Control
strategyName: Zones-Based Calm Down Corner / Regulation Station
briefOverview: Designated classroom space with Zones visuals and regulation tools where students identify their zone, select a strategy, use it, and return to instruction at Green Zone. Proactive and non-punitive — distinct from time-out.
evidenceBaseSummary: Trauma-informed practices (Craig, 2016); sensory integration (Ayres, 1972); Kuypers (2011); Barrett et al. (2015, Building & Environment); Simonsen et al. (2008) classroom management evidence base.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Intellectual Disability
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Set up space in low-traffic area (30-60 min). 2) Stock with Zones materials, timer, sensory tools, breathing cards. 3) Teach 5-step routine (15-20 min + 2-3 practices). 4) Practice during calm moments. 5) Monitor use via log (5 min/week). Limit to 5-7 min consecutive.
resourcesNeeded: Defined space (corner/alcove), Zones check-in visual, Time Timer, 3-5 sensory tools rotated monthly, comfortable seating. Introduce to whole class to reduce stigma.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Multiple students: limit 1-2 + desk-based mini kit. Avoids work: time limit + check-out + complete missed work. Seen as unfair: make universal. Gets messy: assign weekly station manager.
caseStudySummary: Elijah, 1st grader ADHD+anxiety, cried during math. Station with beanbag, timer, breathing cards, fidgets. After 3 practice sessions, independently used belly breathing and returned in 3-5 min. Crying 5/week→≤1.
successCriteriaSummary: Uses routine independently 4/5 times within 6 weeks; time dysregulated decreases 50%+; self-reports Green Zone before return 75%+; 30% fewer ODRs.

author: Sally Shaywitz
functionalNeedAreas: Reading, Phonological Processing, Literacy
briefOverview: Explicit instruction in hearing, isolating, blending, segmenting, and manipulating individual speech sounds (phonemes) in spoken words. Targets the core phonological deficit underlying dyslexia. Begins orally without print.
evidenceBaseSummary: Shaywitz (2020); National Reading Panel (2000); Connecticut Longitudinal Study; fMRI showing neural reading circuit changes (Shaywitz et al., 2004, Biological Psychiatry).
applicableDiagnoses: Specific Learning Disabilities (Dyslexia); Speech/Language Impairment
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess baseline with PAST screener (15 min). 2) Sequence skills: rhyme→syllable→onset-rime→phoneme isolation→blending→segmentation→manipulation. 3) Explicit instruction (10-15 min daily). 4) Manipulatives — Elkonin boxes (embedded). 5) Practice with corrective feedback (5-8 min daily). 6) Progress monitor weekly (3 min).
resourcesNeeded: Elkonin boxes and colored tokens, PSF probes (DIBELS/AIMSweb), quiet space. Keep purely oral — sound before print. Support: reading specialist, SLP, or trained aide.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Confuses similar phonemes: mirrors + tactile cues. Older student finds it babyish: age-appropriate words + explain neuroscience. Limited time: embed 5-min warm-ups.
caseStudySummary: Marcus, 2nd grader dyslexia, couldn't segment individual phonemes. Daily 12-min Elkonin box sessions. PSF score 8→27 CPM in 3 weeks. Began sounding out unfamiliar words for first time.
successCriteriaSummary: At/above PSF benchmark (35+ CPM); 80% accuracy segmenting 4-phoneme words; transfer to sounding out unknown words in text.

author: Sally Shaywitz
functionalNeedAreas: Reading, Writing, Literacy
briefOverview: Simultaneous visual-auditory-kinesthetic-tactile engagement to teach letter-sound correspondences and decoding patterns. Student sees, says, and traces/writes simultaneously. Shaywitz identifies OG methodology as the gold standard.
evidenceBaseSummary: Shaywitz (2020) Ch. 16-20, 22; IDA Knowledge & Practice Standards; Yale neuroimaging showing OG normalizes neural reading pathways; Orton-Gillingham tradition since 1930s.
applicableDiagnoses: Specific Learning Disabilities (Dyslexia); ADHD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select target phonics pattern from scope/sequence (2 min). 2) VAK drill: show card, say sound, trace in sand/air, write while saying (5 min). 3) Blending drill: tap, say, sweep (5 min). 4) Dictation: sounds→words→sentences (5-8 min). 5) Controlled decodable text reading (5-8 min). 6) Cumulative review (3 min). 4-5x/week, 30-45 min.
resourcesNeeded: Letter tiles/magnetic letters, sand tray, decodable readers, sound-spelling cards. Instructor MUST be trained in structured literacy (OG, Wilson, LETRS).
challengesSolutionsSummary: Memorizes sight words: use novel nonsense words. Fatigue: shorter segments with movement breaks. Teacher not OG-trained: deliver through specialist.
caseStudySummary: Amira, 4th grader, only read CVC words. Wilson Reading 4x/week → mastered short vowels and digraphs by lesson 12. Correctly decoded 'shrimp' during dictation. Teacher noticed tapping under syllables during science.
successCriteriaSummary: 90%+ accuracy on taught pattern words; 85%+ dictated words; applies decoding not guessing (running record); 1+ standard score/month on WJ Word Attack.

author: Sally Shaywitz
functionalNeedAreas: Reading, Fluency, Comprehension
briefOverview: Structured repeated reading of the same passage with a fluent model to build speed, accuracy, and prosody. Student reads passage multiple times over several days with feedback and self-charting. Bridges decoding and comprehension.
evidenceBaseSummary: Shaywitz (2020) Ch. 15; Samuels (1979) repeated reading research; NRP — guided repeated oral reading with feedback improves fluency and comprehension.
applicableDiagnoses: Specific Learning Disabilities (Dyslexia); ADHD; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select instructional-level passage, 90-95% accuracy (5 min/week). 2) Cold read + baseline WCPM (2 min). 3) Fluent model read (2 min). 4) Paired reading — unison then solo (3 min). 5) Independent re-reads x2-3 (3-4 min each). 6) Final timed read + chart WCPM (2 min). Daily, 12-15 min. Same passage 3-4 days.
resourcesNeeded: Leveled passages (Read Naturally, DIBELS), timer, student fluency graph. Don't use too-difficult passages — below 90% causes frustration. Reader's Theater for engagement.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Ignores prosody: mark phrase boundaries, rate expression 1-4. Finds repetition boring: high-interest passages, scripts, jokes. Home practice without feedback: one-page protocol + video.
caseStudySummary: Jaylen, 3rd grader dyslexia, 42 WCPM (benchmark 107). Daily repeated reading: Monday cold 48→Friday same passage 78. After 8 weeks, novel-passage cold read 71 WCPM. Voluntarily reading during independent time for first time.
successCriteriaSummary: Cold-read WCPM increases 1-2/week; prosody 3+ on NAEP scale; comprehension retell 80%+; student reports increased confidence.

author: Sally Shaywitz
functionalNeedAreas: Reading, Assessment, Academic Access
briefOverview: Ensures tests measure actual knowledge rather than the disability. Extended time, oral administration, separate setting, alternative response formats. Shaywitz argues this is a civil right and equity issue, not an unfair advantage.
evidenceBaseSummary: Shaywitz (2020) Ch. 19-21; neuroimaging — phonological deficit with intact reasoning/conceptual systems; Lewandowski et al. (2003); IDEA, Section 504, ADA.
applicableDiagnoses: Specific Learning Disabilities (Dyslexia/Dysgraphia); ADHD; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Document in IEP/504 (annual meeting). 2) Prepare testing environment (10 min). 3) Administer with 1.5x extended time (adjust to 2x if needed). 4) Offer oral administration. 5) Provide alternative response formats. 6) Review quarterly.
resourcesNeeded: Quiet separate space + proctor, audio recordings or test reader, speech-to-text software, word processor with spell-check. Use routinely in classroom, not just high-stakes.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Teacher sees as unfair: eyeglasses analogy + PD from Ch. 19. Student refuses (stigma): normalize quiet testing for all; explain neuroscience. Inconsistent implementation: accommodation summary card to all teachers.
caseStudySummary: Sofia, 7th grader dyslexia, Ds/Fs on social studies despite strong discussions. With 1.5x time + oral administration: B+. Teacher: 'The test was the barrier, not her knowledge.' Semester average 58%→84%.
successCriteriaSummary: Scores align within 10-15% of observed performance; completes all items; reduced test anxiety (1-5 scale); meets content-area IEP goals.

author: Sally Shaywitz
functionalNeedAreas: Reading, Content-Area Learning, Vocabulary
briefOverview: Provides audio versions of assigned readings so students access grade-level content through listening while decoding skills develop. TTS reads digital text aloud. Shaywitz calls these essential 'bypass' strategies — not cheating, but a legitimate parallel input channel.
evidenceBaseSummary: Shaywitz (2020) Ch. 19-21; 'sea of strengths' model; neuroimaging — dyslexic readers listening to complex text activate same comprehension networks as typical readers. Learning Ally and Bookshare research-supported.
applicableDiagnoses: Specific Learning Disabilities (Dyslexia); Visual Impairment; ADHD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Register with Learning Ally/Bookshare (30-60 min setup). 2) Enable device TTS (15 min). 3) Provide digital versions of all assigned texts. 4) Train student on tools (2-3 sessions, 15 min each). 5) Pair listening with visual text tracking. 6) Integrate daily across content areas.
resourcesNeeded: Learning Ally membership, Bookshare (free for qualifying students), device with headphones, Voice Dream Reader or Immersive Reader. Start at 1.0x speed, increase to 1.5-2x.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Doesn't follow text: use synchronized highlighting apps. Text unavailable: OCR scan. Stigma: normalize audiobooks for whole class. Tech failures: backup headphones + offline downloads.
caseStudySummary: David, 5th grader, 2nd-grade reading but 6th-grade listening comprehension. Listened to Number the Stars via Learning Ally + Kindle text. Most insightful class discussion contributor. Oral comprehension assessment: 92%.
successCriteriaSummary: 100% assigned texts accessed via audio/TTS; content-area scores 75%+ (accommodated); grade-level vocabulary acquisition; independent tool initiation within 4-6 weeks.

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation, Executive Function, Communication
strategyName: Plan B Conversation (Collaborative and Proactive Solutions)
briefOverview: Three-step collaborative problem-solving conversation: (1) Empathy — gather the student's concern, (2) Define Adult Concerns — share your concern, (3) Invitation — brainstorm a mutually satisfactory solution. Replaces punitive responses with skill-building collaboration.
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene (2008) 'kids do well if they can'; Pollastri et al. (2016, Harvard Review of Psychiatry); Greene & Ablon (2006) reduced seclusion/restraint; Greene et al. (2006, Psychiatric Services).
applicableDiagnoses: All diagnoses — universal across ADHD, ASD, Anxiety, Depression, LD, Speech/Language, Intellectual Disability
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Identify unsolved problem in observable terms (5 min). 2) Empathy Step: neutral opening + reflective listening (5-10 min). 3) Define Adult Concerns without blame (2-3 min). 4) Invitation: brainstorm together (5-10 min). 5) Follow up within 1 week (2-3 min). Recycle if needed.
resourcesNeeded: Quiet private setting, written list of unsolved problems (from ALSUP), visual supports for limited verbal skills. School psychologist can model early conversations. Don't rush — empathy step has the critical info.
challengesSolutionsSummary: One-word answers: drilling strategies ('Can you say more?'). Teacher jumps to solutions: practice empathy in colleague role-plays. Time constraints: split across two conversations.
caseStudySummary: Marcus, 4th grader ADHD, threw pencil during writing. Plan B revealed he 'never knows what to write about' and hand 'gets tired.' Solution: graphic organizer + Chromebook typing. Pencil-throwing daily→zero; completed 4 assignments.
successCriteriaSummary: Reduced frequency/intensity of target behavior; student articulates solution; solution durable 2+ weeks; improved student-teacher relationship.

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function, Behavior Regulation, Communication
strategyName: ALSUP (Assessment of Lagging Skills and Unsolved Problems)
briefOverview: Structured team-based assessment identifying cognitive skills the student lacks (lagging skills) and specific situations where deficits manifest (unsolved problems). Reframes 'defiance' as skill deficit meeting unmet demand. Foundation for all CPS interventions.
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene (2008); Greene & Ablon (2006); executive function research (Barkley, 1997; Zelazo & Carlson, 2012). Free tool from Lives in the Balance; used in hundreds of schools.
applicableDiagnoses: All diagnoses — universal assessment tool
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Download ALSUP from livesinthebalance.org (5 min). 2) Convene team: teacher, SPED, counselor, parent (30-45 min). 3) Identify lagging skills — if anyone sees it, check it (10-15 min). 4) Identify unsolved problems in 'Difficulty [X] during [context]' format (15-20 min). 5) Prioritize max 3 for Plan B (5-10 min). 6) Revisit monthly.
resourcesNeeded: ALSUP form (free), meeting space, 30-45 min collaborative time, student input when appropriate. Parents often provide critical invisible-at-school information.
challengesSolutionsSummary: Team says 'he just doesn't want to': redirect to 'What skill would they need?' Too many problems: only 3 active via Plan B. Disagreement: ALSUP is additive, not voted on.
caseStudySummary: Aisha, 6th grader anxiety+reading LD. Lagging skills: managing frustration, transitions, black-and-white thinking. Prioritized reading comprehension and feedback for Plan B; lunch transition to Plan C.
successCriteriaSummary: Completed with 3+ perspectives + student input; observable skill-based language; max 3 active targets; revisited monthly with problems solved over time.

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation, Executive Function, Self-Regulation
briefOverview: Conducts the Plan B conversation BEFORE predictable challenging behavior occurs, during a calm period. Leverages full cognitive capacity because the student is not already dysregulated. Preferred mode of Plan B implementation.
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene (2008); Arnsten (2009, Nature Reviews Neuroscience) — prefrontal cortex impaired during arousal; school CPS implementations (Greene & Ablon, 2006) — majority of successes from proactive conversations.
applicableDiagnoses: All diagnoses with recurring behavioral challenges
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Consult ALSUP for top priority (5 min). 2) Schedule calm time: before school, check-in, lunch (2-3 min planning). 3) Empathy step proactively (5-10 min). 4) Define concern (2-3 min). 5) Invite collaborative solutions (5-10 min). 6) Document + set follow-up (2 min). 7) Follow up in 3-5 days.
resourcesNeeded: Completed ALSUP, consistent private meeting time, tracking sheet for solutions/follow-ups. For nonverbal students: visual supports, social stories. NEVER attempt when student is escalated.
challengesSolutionsSummary: No time: use morning meetings, advisory, lunch, arrival. Student doesn't see a problem: deeper drilling. First solution fails: expected — return to empathy with curiosity.
caseStudySummary: Jayden, 3rd grader ASD, melted down Tuesday/Thursday for specials. Monday morning proactive Plan B: he didn't know when they'd leave or what to bring. Solution: visual schedule card with times/supplies. Meltdowns 2x/week→zero in one month.
successCriteriaSummary: Conversations during calm periods; solutions documented; targeted problem reduced within 2-4 weeks; student initiates problem-solving language.

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation, Emotional Regulation
briefOverview: Deliberate systematic reduction of imposed adult will (Plan A) — demands backed by rewards/consequences expecting immediate compliance. Greene argues Plan A is the #1 trigger of challenging behavior. Shift to Plan B (collaborate) and Plan C (deprioritize). Only safety issues warrant Plan A.
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene (2008); self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 2000); Skiba et al. (2011) — punitive methods disproportionately affect students with disabilities without improving behavior.
applicableDiagnoses: All diagnoses where behavioral challenges persist despite traditional approaches
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Audit Plan A responses over 2-3 days (15-20 min). 2) Categorize each: Plan B or Plan C? Only safety = Plan A. 3) Shift items to B or C (ongoing). 4) Replace in-moment Plan A with Emergency Plan B (3-5 min/incident). 5) Monitor ratio weekly (5 min).
resourcesNeeded: Tracking log (Demand/Response/Plan Used), coaching from behavior specialist or CPS-trained peer. Reducing Plan A ≠ being permissive — it's being strategic.
challengesSolutionsSummary: 'Lose control?': Plan A creates illusion of control while producing power struggles. Ingrained habits: start with one student, one problem. Pressure to 'be firm': share outcome data (fewer referrals, suspensions).
caseStudySummary: Ms. Torres used Plan A with DeShawn (ADHD+anxiety) 12x/day. Audit showed most were low-stakes. Moved 'complete all 20 problems' to Plan C (accepted 10). Plan B for staying seated → standing table. Outbursts daily→2x/week; Plan A <2x/day by week 3.
successCriteriaSummary: Documented Plan A reduction; decreased power struggles and referrals; increased Plan B/C ratio; reduced oppositional behavior; teacher reports greater efficacy.

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation, Emotional Regulation, Relationship Building
briefOverview: Intentional decision to set aside low-priority expectations temporarily until higher-priority problems are solved via Plan B. Not ignoring or permissiveness — calculated triage that reduces demand load, preserves the relationship, and creates bandwidth for Plan B success.
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene (2008); cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988); therapeutic alliance research (Karver et al., 2006) — relationship quality is strongest predictor of positive outcomes.
applicableDiagnoses: All diagnoses where demand overwhelm contributes to behavioral challenges
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Review ALSUP (5 min). 2) Select top 2-3 for Plan B (5 min). 3) Designate rest as Plan C — write them down (5 min). 4) Communicate to ALL adults working with student (5-10 min). 5) Implement: do not impose Plan C expectations (ongoing). 6) Revisit/rotate monthly as Plan B solves problems.
resourcesNeeded: Completed ALSUP with prioritization, written Plan C list shared with all staff/paras/subs. Communicate rationale to parents. Hardest strategy — requires tolerating imperfection. Often produces immediate relief.
challengesSolutionsSummary: 'Giving in?': strategic triage like ER. Others notice: class fairness discussion. Staff inconsistency: clear documentation. Parent pushback: explain triage metaphor.
caseStudySummary: Sophia, 2nd grader anxiety+SLI, 7 unsolved problems. Top 2 for Plan B: morning work + cafeteria noise. 5 others Plan C (hand-raising, seating, group reading). Crying stopped in 3 weeks. By month 2, group reading moved to Plan B — agreed to read with partner. Incidents 6/day→<1.
successCriteriaSummary: Written Plan C list shared with staff; no power struggles on Plan C items; decreased overall stress/incidents; Plan B more productive; Plan C items rotate to Plan B over time.

bookTitle: 12 Principles for Raising a Child with ADHD
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior regulation; Self-monitoring; Impulse control; Transition management
briefOverview: This strategy is drawn from Barkley's core principle of being a "shepherd" rather than a reactive disciplinarian. The adult anticipates challenging situations before they occur and puts supports, cues, and environmental modifications in place ahead of time, rather than waiting to correct misbehavior
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley's 12 Principles framework is built on his neuropsychological model of ADHD as a disorder of self-regulation and executive function (Barkley, 2020, Chapter 1). The shepherding concept draws from antecedent-based intervention research showing that proactive strategies reduce problem behavior b
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Identify high-risk situations; Develop a proactive plan for each situation; Pre-teach expectations before each target situation; Position proximity strategically; Provide transition bridges; Debrief and adjust
resourcesNeeded: Behavioral tracking sheet or simple tally chart for high-risk situations; Visual schedule posted in the classroom and/or on the student's desk; Nonverbal cue system agreed upon between teacher and student (e.g., a hand signal meaning "check yourself"); Tip: Frame this as "setting the student up for success," not surveillance; Adaptation for older students: Involve the student in identifying their own high-risk times and co-creating the proactive plan
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Teacher feels this requires too much individual attention*: Start with just the top 2 most problematic situations per day. As routines become automatic, the time investment decreases significantly. Many proactive strategies (proximity, pre-teaching) take under 30 seconds. - *Student resists being
caseStudySummary: Jaylen is a 3rd-grade student with ADHD, Combined Presentation. He frequently has behavioral incidents during transitions, particularly moving from preferred activities (art, recess) to non-preferred ones (math, writing). His teacher, Ms. Alvarez, identified three daily transitions as high-risk thro
successCriteriaSummary: Reduction of behavioral incidents during targeted high-risk situations by at least 50% within 4 weeks; Student successfully transitions between activities with no more than 1 verbal prompt on 80% of occasions; Teacher documents proactive strategies used and rates fidelity of implementation weekly at 80% or higher; Data source: Daily behavior tracking log with incident frequency by time of day and activity type

bookTitle: 12 Principles for Raising a Child with ADHD
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional regulation; Frustration tolerance; Impulse control; Self-monitoring of internal states
strategyName: Emotion Coaching and Self-Regulation Pause Protocol
briefOverview: Based on Barkley's principle that ADHD fundamentally impairs emotional self-regulation, this strategy teaches the adult to serve as an external emotional regulator for the child. When the student begins to show signs of emotional escalation (frustration, anger, excitement), the teacher uses a struct
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) dedicates Principle 5 to emotional regulation, arguing that emotional impulsivity is one of the most impairing yet underrecognized features of ADHD. He draws on his Emotion Impulsivity model (Barkley, 2015, *Emotional Dysregulation in ADHD*) and research by Surman et al. (2013) showin
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Establish the regulatory space; Teach emotional vocabulary; Detect early escalation signs; Implement the Pause Protocol; Guide re-entry; Track and review patterns; Gradually build student self-initiation
resourcesNeeded: Emotion/regulation zone with sensory tools (fidgets, headphones, calm-down visual cards, timer); Visual emotion scale (Zones of Regulation chart, 5-point scale, or feeling thermometer); Emotion tracking log or simple daily check-in form; Tip: The tone of the adult is critical — remain calm, warm, and matter-of-fact. Any hint of frustration or sarcasm will escalate the student further.; Adaptation for teens: Replace the physical "reset zone" with permission to use a brief hallway walk, listen to one song on headphones, or step to a designated area. Teens respond better to autonomy-preserving language.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student refuses to use the reset area or denies feeling upset*: Do not force the issue. Offer the alternative choice: "That's okay. Would you like to just skip this problem and come back to it?" Forcing engagement during escalation is counterproductive. - *Other students ask why one student gets
caseStudySummary: Amara is a 5th-grade student with ADHD and comorbid anxiety. She experiences intense frustration during math, often crumpling papers, putting her head down, or crying. Her teacher, Mr. Chen, implemented the Emotion Coaching protocol. He introduced a 5-point feeling thermometer at Amara's desk and ta
successCriteriaSummary: Reduction in emotional escalation episodes (reaching level 4 or 5) by 50% within 6 weeks; Student self-initiates regulatory breaks (without teacher prompting) on at least 3 out of 5 occasions by week 8; Recovery time from escalation decreases from baseline (e.g., from 15 minutes to under 5 minutes); Data source: Emotion episode tracking log recording trigger, intensity, strategy used, recovery time, and whether the break was teacher-initiated or student-initiated

bookTitle: 12 Principles for Raising a Child with ADHD
functionalNeedAreas: Working memory; Rule internalization; Self-monitoring; Task initiation and follow-through
strategyName: Rule Externalization Through Physical Cue Cards
briefOverview: Drawing on Barkley's principle that children with ADHD "know what to do but can't do what they know," this strategy externalizes rules, expectations, and behavioral steps by placing them in the physical environment at the exact point where the behavior is needed. Rather than relying on the student t
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020) emphasizes across multiple principles that the core problem in ADHD is not knowledge but performance — the gap between knowing a rule and executing it at the appropriate moment (Principle 7). This aligns with his executive function model (Barkley, 1997, 2012) in which internalized spe
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the 3-5 key behavioral or procedural rules the student struggles to follow; Create personalized cue cards; Place cards at the point of performance; Teach the student to use the cards; Prompt card use initially; Review and update cards
resourcesNeeded: Index cards or cardstock, laminator (or clear packing tape as laminate substitute); Velcro strips for attaching/detaching cards from desks or folders; Small icons or clip art for visual support (free from sites like ARASAAC or Boardmaker); Tip: Keep cards brief — no more than 5 words per step. Lengthy cards will not be read.; Adaptation for older students: Use a small printed checklist inside a binder or as a phone/tablet lock screen rather than desk-taped cards, which may feel stigmatizing
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student ignores the cards*: Cards alone are passive. Pair with a reinforcement system: "Each time I see you check your card before starting, you earn a point." Active reference must be reinforced initially. - *Too many cards become overwhelming*: Limit to 3 active cards at any time. Prioritize th
caseStudySummary: Devin is a 2nd-grade student with ADHD, Predominantly Inattentive Presentation. He consistently forgets procedural steps: he turns in papers without his name, starts assignments before reading directions, and leaves materials at his desk when transitioning to small groups. His teacher, Ms. Rodriguez
successCriteriaSummary: Student independently references cue card before beginning targeted activity on 80% of occasions within 4 weeks; Target behavior compliance (e.g., name on paper, following multi-step directions) increases to 80% accuracy within 6 weeks; At least one cue card is "retired" (mastered) within the first grading period; Data source: Teacher checklist tracking whether the student referenced the card and whether the target behavior was performed correctly, sampled 3 times daily

bookTitle: 12 Principles for Raising a Child with ADHD
functionalNeedAreas: Sustained attention; Motivation with delayed rewards; Self-monitoring; Task persistence
strategyName: Accountability Through Immediate and Specific Feedback Loops
briefOverview: Barkley's principle that consequences must be more immediate, more frequent, and more salient for children with ADHD informs this strategy. Instead of waiting until the end of a lesson, assignment, or day to provide feedback, the teacher creates structured micro-feedback loops — brief, specific stat
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020, Principles 8 and 9) argues that the ADHD brain has a fundamentally altered sensitivity to reinforcement, requiring consequences that are more immediate and more frequent than typical peers need. This is supported by the delay aversion model (Sonuga-Barke, 2002) and reinforcement sensi
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Set a feedback interval; Define what specific behaviors to monitor; Deliver micro-feedback at each interval; Use a 4:1 positive-to-corrective ratio; Pair feedback with a visual progress tracker; Gradually extend the interval
resourcesNeeded: Silent interval timer (vibrating watch, phone app like "Interval Timer," or a smartwatch); Visual progress tracking card on the student's desk (simple grid of boxes); Tip: Feedback must be specific and behavioral, not vague praise. "Good job" is far less effective than "You stayed focused through that whole paragraph."; Adaptation for large classrooms: Use a roving check-in pattern where you pass by the student's desk at intervals during independent work, delivering feedback in a quick whisper; Adaptation for older students: Shift to written feedback on sticky notes placed on the student's work, or use a shared digital document with brief comments
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Teacher cannot maintain the feedback interval with other students needing attention*: Start with just one work period per day (e.g., independent math time). Use a paraprofessional or teaching aide during other periods if available. Even one consistent feedback period daily produces results. - *St
caseStudySummary: Sofia is a 4th-grade student with ADHD, Combined Presentation. During independent work, she typically completes only 20-30% of assignments, spending most of her time looking around, chatting, or doodling. Her teacher, Mr. Williams, implemented a 7-minute feedback loop during daily independent readin
successCriteriaSummary: Student's on-task behavior during targeted work periods increases to 75% of intervals within 4 weeks; Assignment completion rate increases by at least 30 percentage points from baseline within 6 weeks; Feedback interval is successfully extended by at least 5 minutes from baseline within 8 weeks; Data source: Interval-based on-task recording sheet with check marks per interval, assignment completion percentage log

bookTitle: 12 Principles for Raising a Child with ADHD
functionalNeedAreas: Sustained attention; Hyperactivity/motor restlessness; Executive function (working memory, inhibition); Arousal regulation
strategyName: Physical Exercise and Motor Activity Integration Before Demanding Tasks
briefOverview: Barkley dedicates a full principle to the importance of physical health and exercise for managing ADHD, noting that physical activity has direct effects on the same neurotransmitter systems (dopamine, norepinephrine) targeted by ADHD medications. This strategy involves deliberately scheduling brief
evidenceBaseSummary: Barkley (2020, Principle 12) cites research showing that 20-30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise improves executive function performance in children with ADHD comparably to a low dose of stimulant medication (Pontifex, Saliba, Raine, Picchietti, & Hillman, 2013). A meta-analysis by Cerrillo-Urbin
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the most cognitively demanding period of the day; Schedule 10-15 minutes of moderate physical activity immediately before that period; Ensure the activity is aerobic and sustained; Provide a 2-3 minute cool-down transition; Begin the demanding task immediately after the cool-down; Track performance on demanding tasks
resourcesNeeded: Timer for activity duration; Space for physical activity (hallway, gym, outdoor area, or classroom with desks pushed aside); Movement activity options appropriate for the setting (list of exercises, movement video access, physical education equipment); Tip: The activity does not need to be a formal "exercise" — active chores, walking errands, or active play all work if they sustain elevated heart rate; Adaptation for limited space: Desk-based exercises (chair push-ups, seated marching, resistance band pulls) can provide some benefit but are less effective than full-body aerobic activity
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Schedule constraints prevent adding exercise before the target period*: Advocate for schedule modifications during IEP meetings. Alternatively, build 5-minute movement breaks into the first 5 minutes of the target class period itself — even brief activity helps. - *Student is resistant to exercis
caseStudySummary: Elijah is a 6th-grade student with ADHD who takes medication but still struggles significantly during his 5th-period math class (1:00-1:45 PM), when medication effects are waning. His IEP team implemented a physical activity protocol: Elijah spends the last 12 minutes of his lunch period (12:45-12:5
successCriteriaSummary: Student engages in 10+ minutes of moderate physical activity before the target period on at least 4 out of 5 school days; On-task behavior during the target period increases by at least 20 percentage points from baseline within 4 weeks; Assignment completion rate during the target period increases by at least 20 percentage points from baseline; Data source: Activity log (duration and type of exercise) paired with on-task interval recording and assignment completion tracking during the target period

bookTitle: Executive Skills in Children and Adolescents (3rd ed.)
functionalNeedAreas: Executive function (any or all subtypes); Organization; Planning and prioritization; Attention and task persistence
strategyName: Executive Skills Profile Assessment and Targeted Intervention Matching
briefOverview: Dawson and Guare provide a systematic approach to identifying a student's specific executive skill strengths and weaknesses through structured questionnaires and observation protocols. Rather than treating all executive function difficulties the same, this strategy uses their assessment tools to cre
evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2018, Chapters 2-4) developed their executive skills framework based on the developmental neuropsychology literature, particularly Luria's (1973) model of frontal lobe functions, Barkley's (1997) model of ADHD and executive function, and Gioia et al.'s (2000) Behavior Rating Invent
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Administer the Executive Skills Questionnaire; If age-appropriate, administer the student self-rating; Create the Executive Skills Profile; Select interventions matched to the weakest skills; Implement no more than 2 interventions at a time; Reassess the profile every 8-10 weeks
resourcesNeeded: Dawson and Guare's Executive Skills Questionnaires (available in the book's appendices and some are available at smartbutscatteredkids.com); Scoring guide and profile template; Intervention selection matrix (book's Part 2 chapters organized by executive skill); Tip: Share the profile with the student (age-appropriately) — students who understand their own profile become better self-advocates; Adaptation for IEP teams: Use the profile to write more specific Present Levels of Performance and to select accommodations that target documented skill deficits rather than generic supports
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Raters disagree significantly on the profile*: This is common and informative. Disagreement often reflects that the student performs differently across settings. Use the discrepancies to identify which environmental factors support or hinder the student. Interview raters to understand context. -
caseStudySummary: Nina is a 7th-grade student with ADHD and a specific learning disability in written expression. Her teachers report that she is "disorganized, never starts work on time, and doesn't seem to care about grades." The school psychologist administered Dawson and Guare's Executive Skills Questionnaires to
successCriteriaSummary: Completion of comprehensive Executive Skills Profile with input from at least 2 raters within first 2 weeks; Targeted executive skill ratings improve by at least 1 point (on a 5-point scale) within 10 weeks; Functional outcomes tied to the targeted skills (e.g., assignment completion, on-time task starts) improve by at least 25% within one grading period; Data source: Executive Skills Questionnaire scores at baseline and reassessment, paired with functional outcome data (missing assignments, grades, behavioral observations)

bookTitle: Executive Skills in Children and Adolescents (3rd ed.)
functionalNeedAreas: Organization; Planning/prioritization; Working memory; Sustained attention; Task initiation
strategyName: Environmental Modification and Task Demand Reduction
briefOverview: Dawson and Guare organize their intervention framework into three categories: modifying the environment, modifying the task, and teaching the skill. This strategy focuses on the first two — changing the physical and procedural environment to reduce the executive function demands on the student, rath
evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2018, Chapter 5) present environmental modification as the first line of intervention, based on the principle that changing the environment is faster and more reliable than teaching new skills, especially for students whose executive skills are significantly below developmental exp
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Conduct an environmental and task demand audit; Identify the top 3-5 modifiable demands; Implement environmental modifications; Implement task modifications; Monitor whether modifications are sufficient or if skill-building is also needed; Document modifications in the IEP
resourcesNeeded: Graphic organizer templates (narrative, expository, compare/contrast, etc.); Privacy folders or desk carrels; Noise-reducing headphones; Pre-formatted assignment sheets (with spaces, numbering, and graphic cues already built in); Tip: Modifications are not "lowering expectations." The academic standard remains the same; the delivery vehicle changes. A student who completes 15 problems correctly demonstrates the same mastery as one who completes 30.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Teachers worry that modifications are "unfair" to other students or "enabling"*: Explain that accommodations level the playing field — they provide access to the standard without reducing rigor. Use the analogy of eyeglasses: they modify the environment to correct a visual deficit, not to provide
caseStudySummary: Carlos is a 4th-grade student with ADHD and a specific learning disability in reading comprehension. He becomes overwhelmed during language arts, often refusing to start reading passages. An environmental and task audit revealed: (1) his desk faced the hallway window (visual distraction), (2) readin
successCriteriaSummary: At least 3 environmental or task modifications implemented within the first week; Task refusal or avoidance behavior decreases by at least 50% within 4 weeks; Work completion rate increases by at least 30 percentage points within 6 weeks; Data source: Task refusal tracking log, assignment completion records, and periodic teacher checklist confirming modifications are in place

bookTitle: Executive Skills in Children and Adolescents (3rd ed.)
functionalNeedAreas: Planning and prioritization; Metacognition (self-awareness of one's own thinking); Goal-directed persistence; Task initiation; Organization
briefOverview: This strategy teaches students a four-step metacognitive routine they apply to any academic task: (1) set a Goal for the work session, (2) make a Plan for how to accomplish it, (3) Do the work while monitoring progress, and (4) Review what was accomplished and what needs to happen next. Dawson and G
evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2018, Chapters 6 and 15) present Goal-Plan-Do-Review as a core intervention for building planning, prioritization, and metacognitive skills. The framework draws from Meichenbaum's (1977) cognitive-behavioral self-instruction training, which demonstrated that explicitly teaching stu
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Introduce the GPDR framework; Model the routine with think-alouds; Guide the student through GPDR with prompts; Provide a GPDR worksheet or template; Fade teacher prompting over 4-6 weeks; Generalize across subjects; Review GPDR logs weekly
resourcesNeeded: GPDR visual poster for the classroom and desk-sized card for the student; GPDR worksheets (simple 4-box format); Timer (for monitoring during the "Do" phase); Tip: The "Review" step is the most commonly skipped and arguably the most important. Build it into the class routine (e.g., "Everyone, take 1 minute to review your work before packing up").; Adaptation for younger students (K-2): Simplify to "What am I going to do? How will I do it? Did I do it?" with picture supports
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student rushes through the Goal and Plan steps to get to "doing"*: Require the goal and plan to be written (or stated to the teacher) before the student is allowed to begin. Use a "planning ticket" — the student writes the plan on an index card and shows it to the teacher before starting. - *Stud
caseStudySummary: Isaiah is an 8th-grade student with ADHD and a specific learning disability in math. He begins assignments impulsively, working problems in random order, skipping directions, and often running out of time. His resource room teacher, Ms. Patel, introduced the GPDR framework. Each day at the start of
successCriteriaSummary: Student completes a GPDR worksheet independently (without teacher prompting) before and after work sessions on 80% of occasions within 6 weeks; Work completion rate increases by at least 25% from baseline within one grading period; Student's self-set goals become more realistic over time (goal-actual discrepancy decreases by 50% within 8 weeks); Data source: Completed GPDR worksheets collected weekly, assignment completion logs, and comparison of planned vs. actual output

bookTitle: Executive Skills in Children and Adolescents (3rd ed.)
functionalNeedAreas: Task initiation; Sustained attention (particularly getting started); Planning; Response to non-preferred tasks
briefOverview: Task initiation is one of the most commonly impaired executive skills in students with ADHD, and Dawson and Guare dedicate specific interventions to it. This strategy provides a structured, repeatable routine that the student follows every time they need to start a task, eliminating the ambiguity an
evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2018, Chapter 8) identify task initiation as a distinct executive skill and one of the most impactful targets for intervention. Research on task initiation difficulties in ADHD links them to deficits in activation energy — the neurological threshold needed to shift from a resting o
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Create the Task Initiation Card; Teach the routine explicitly; Practice with guided support; Fade to gestural and visual prompts; Fade to independent use; Track initiation latency; Generalize to homework and other settings
resourcesNeeded: Laminated Task Initiation Card (desk-sized); Visual timer (Time Timer, phone timer, or classroom timer visible to the student); Initiation latency tracking sheet (teacher records start-of-task time vs. student-begins-working time); Tip: The most critical element is identifying the "first step." Students with task initiation difficulty are often paralyzed by the size of the whole task. Helping them see just the first tiny action ("open your book to page 42") dramatically reduces the barrier.; Adaptation for younger students: Reduce the routine to 3 steps with pictures: "Clear, Look, Start."
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student completes the routine but still doesn't start working*: The "first step" may still be too large. Help the student break it down further. If the first step is "read the passage," change it to "read the first paragraph" or "read the title and first sentence." - *Student loses or ignores the
caseStudySummary: Leah is a 5th-grade student with ADHD, Predominantly Inattentive Presentation. Her primary difficulty is task initiation — she will sit at her desk for 10-20 minutes before beginning any assignment, sharpening pencils, rearranging materials, or simply staring. Her average initiation latency (time fr
successCriteriaSummary: Task initiation latency decreases by at least 50% from baseline within 4 weeks; Student independently uses the initiation routine (without verbal prompting) on 80% of occasions within 6 weeks; Assignment completion rate increases by at least 25% from baseline within one grading period; Data source: Task initiation latency log (time assigned vs. time student begins working), tracked for at least 3 tasks per day

bookTitle: Executive Skills in Children and Adolescents (3rd ed.)
functionalNeedAreas: Planning and prioritization; Time management; Organization; Goal-directed persistence; Task initiation on multi-step projects
strategyName: Long-Term Project Planning with Backward Design and Milestone Check-ins
briefOverview: Students with executive function deficits consistently struggle with long-term projects because these require planning, prioritization, time management, organization, and sustained effort over days or weeks — a convergence of nearly all executive skills. Dawson and Guare describe a structured approa
evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2018, Chapters 9-10) present project planning as a critical application of their executive skills framework, noting that long-term projects are the single most common source of academic failure for students with executive function deficits. The backward design approach is supported
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety
implementationStepsSummary: Receive the project assignment and identify the final deadline; Use backward design to identify milestones; Create a visual project timeline; Schedule check-in meetings at each milestone; Provide structured support at each milestone as needed; Conduct a post-project review
resourcesNeeded: Visual project timeline template (paper or digital — Google Calendar, Trello, or a simple table); Calendar or planner where milestone dates are recorded; Milestone check-in form (3 questions: What did you complete? What obstacles came up? What's your plan for the next milestone?); Tip: Set milestone dates conservatively — build in 1-2 buffer days before the final deadline for unexpected delays; Tip: For students who struggle with initiation, make Milestone 1 extremely small and achievable (e.g., "Choose your topic and write it down") to generate early momentum
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student misses a milestone and becomes discouraged*: Adjust the remaining milestones immediately rather than letting the student fall further behind. Say: "Missing one milestone doesn't mean the project is ruined. Let's adjust the plan." Provide extra support for the missed deliverable. - *Teache
caseStudySummary: Marco is a 9th-grade student with ADHD. He has failed to complete 3 out of 4 major projects this school year, each time reporting that he "forgot about it" until the night before. His learning specialist, Dr. Kim, implemented the backward design milestone system for his next project — a history rese
successCriteriaSummary: Student meets at least 75% of intermediate milestones on time within the first project cycle; Project completion rate improves from baseline (e.g., from 25% to 80% of major projects submitted on time); Student can independently identify at least 3 milestones for a new project by the third project cycle; Data source: Milestone tracking sheet with due date, actual completion date, and deliverable quality rating; project submission records

successCriteriaSummary: Student earns at least 70% of available points/check marks daily within 3 weeks; Target executive skill behaviors occur at least 75% of opportunities within 6 weeks; Point requirement is successfully increased at least once within the first grading period (indicating the student is sustaining behavior at a higher demand level); Data source: Daily point tracking card, weekly summary of points earned vs. available, target behavior occurrence log

evidenceBaseSummary: Dawson and Guare (2009, Chapters 4 and 10) discuss working memory as a critical executive skill and describe specific interventions for strengthening it. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; OCD

successCriteriaSummary: Frustration-related meltdowns or shutdowns decrease by at least 60% from baseline within 8 weeks; Student independently selects and uses a Plan B strategy (without teacher prompting) on at least 50% of "stuck" occasions within 6 weeks; Student can name at least 3 alternative strategies for when they are stuck, without looking at the menu, within 4 weeks; Data source: Episode tracking log documenting stuck signals observed, whether Plan B was prompted or self-initiated, which Plan B strategy was chosen, and outcome (returned to work vs. escalated)

successCriteriaSummary: Teen articulates at least one personally meaningful goal within the first 2 sessions; Teen actively participates in check-in meetings (attends, responds, offers input) on at least 80% of scheduled sessions; Progress toward the collaboratively set SMART goal is demonstrated within one grading period (specific metric depends on goal); Data source: Session attendance log, goal progress tracking tool, academic records (GPA, assignment submission rates), and teen self-report of engagement

applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Anxiety; Depression

Ask questions and let the teen do the prioritizing and scheduling. successCriteriaSummary: On-task behavior during cooperative learning increases to 80% or above as measured by momentary time sampling; Task completion rate during group activities reaches 85% or higher; Calling-out or disruptive behavior during group work decreases by 50% from baseline; Data source: Teacher observation checklist completed during each cooperative learning session, with weekly summary graphing

successCriteriaSummary: Note completion rate reaches 90% or above during guided note lessons; Test scores on units using structured notes improve by at least 15 percentage points from baseline; Student independently uses notes as a study tool for at least 2 out of 3 assessments per quarter; Data source: Weekly note completion checks (teacher stamps/initials), gradebook test score comparison, student self-report on study habits

His teacher, Ms. Nguyen, began priming Carlos each morning during the first 4 minutes of homeroom, previewing the key vocabula

successCriteriaSummary: On-task behavior during independent work increases to 75% or above within 4 weeks; Independent work completion rate increases to 80% or above; Frequency of teacher verbal redirections decreases by 50% from baseline; Data source: On-task behavior observations (3 times per week, 5-minute interval sampling), work completion log, teacher redirection tally

bookTitle: How to Reach and Teach Children with ADD/ADHD (2nd ed.)

successCriteriaSummary: Teacher maintains a praise-to-correction ratio of at least 4:1 for the target student for 4 consecutive weeks; Disruptive behavior incidents decrease by 40% from baseline within 4 weeks; Student's on-task behavior increases to 65% or above within 4 weeks; Data source: Teacher self-monitoring of praise ratio (daily paperclip count), frequency count of disruptive behaviors, periodic on-task behavior observation by school psychologist or coach

evidenceBaseSummary: DuPaul and Stoner dedicate an entire chapter section to peer-mediated interventions, citing their own landmark research (DuPaul, Ervin, Hook, & McGoey, 1998) demonstrating that CWPT significantly increased on-task behavior and academic performance in students with ADHD compared to traditional instru

resourcesNeeded: Flashcards or practice materials for each pair; Point sheets for each pair (simple tally format); Timer with audible signal; Team scoreboard (whiteboard or poster); Tip: For the student with ADHD, the tutor role is often more engaging initially because it involves monitoring and providing feedback; start them in this role

successCriteriaSummary: On-task behavior increases to 75% or above within 4 weeks, confirmed by independent observation; Self-monitoring accuracy (agreement between student's rating and independent observer) reaches 80% or above; Academic performance in the target period improves by at least 10 percentage points; Data source: Self-monitoring sheets (daily percentage), independent observer validation (weekly, 10-minute observation), academic grades/quiz scores

successCriteriaSummary: On-task engagement during CAI sessions reaches 90% or above; Skill fluency growth rate increases to at least 2 times the baseline rate; Academic performance on non-technology assessments improves by at least 10 percentage points, demonstrating skill transfer; Data source: Program-generated progress reports (weekly), teacher observation of engagement, classroom assessment scores compared pre/post CAI implementation

resourcesNeeded: Large poster board, whiteboard, or cork board; Sticky notes in multiple colors; Markers or pens; Optional: Trello, Google Calendar, or other digital tool; Tip: Place the board where the teen will see it without trying to — above the desk, on the back of the bedroom door, or next to the mirror

successCriteriaSummary: Missing assignment rate decreases to 2 or fewer per week within 4 weeks; The student independently updates the board at least 4 out of 5 school days; Long-term project milestones are completed on time for at least 80% of interim deadlines; Data source: Missing assignment tracking (from teacher gradebook), student/parent report on board update frequency, project milestone completion log

bookTitle: Teaching Teens with ADD, ADHD & Executive Function Deficits
functionalNeedAreas: Executive function (task initiation, sustained effort, organization); Academic skills (written expression, homework completion); Organization (managing and returning completed work)

implementationStepsSummary: Document the homework challenge with data; Convene a team meeting to establish accommodations; Write accommodations into the IEP or 504 plan; Communicate accommodations to all teachers; Implement a homework time limit at home; Prioritize assignments within the time limit; Review and adjust quarterly
resourcesNeeded: Homework time log for initial data collection (simple grid: subject, start time, end time, completed?); Timer for homework time limit; One-page accommodation summary for teachers; IEP or 504 plan documentation; Tip: Frame the time limit as protecting the teen's mental health and family relationships, not lowering expectations

successCriteriaSummary: Nightly homework time decreases to within 30 minutes of the designated time limit; Homework completion rate increases to 85% or above within 4 weeks; Overall GPA improves or maintains within 0.3 of pre-accommodation level (ensuring accommodations are not lowering performance); Data source: Parent homework time log (weekly), teacher gradebook (homework completion percentage), quarterly GPA review

functionalNeedAreas: Executive function (planning, organizing, prioritizing); Organization (materials management, homework tracking); Academic skills (task completion, study preparation)
briefOverview: The executive function coaching check-in model pairs the teen with ADHD with a designated adult coach (teacher, counselor, mentor, or trained paraprofessional) who conducts brief, structured check-ins at the beginning and end of each school day or at key transition points. resourcesNeeded: Designated coach with 5-10 minutes available at check-in times; Laminated check-in protocol card; Check-in log (simple grid or digital form); The teen's planner or assignment tracking system; Tip: The check-in should feel like a supportive conversation, not an interrogation. Humor and warmth are essential.

successCriteriaSummary: Student independently checks visual schedule before 80% of transitions without adult prompting over 3 consecutive weeks; Transition-related behavioral incidents decrease by 70% from baseline; Average transition time decreases to under 2 minutes; Data source: Daily transition tracking log completed by classroom staff, recording prompt level, transition duration, and behavioral incidents per transition
