successCriteriaSummary: Student achieves 80% or higher independent correct responding on each target skill across 3 consecutive sessions; Student demonstrates mastered skills in at least 2 untrained settings or with 2 untrained people; Rate of skill acquisition increases over time (fewer sessions needed to master new targets); Data source: Trial-by-trial data sheets graphed daily, generalization probes conducted biweekly

evidenceBaseSummary: Moreno and O'Neal recommend structured choice-making as a foundational support for students with ASD. The research base includes Dunlap et al. (1994), who demonstrated that offering choices between academic tasks significantly reduced disruptive behavior and increased engagement in students with dis
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Identify high-resistance activities and transitions; Design choice options for each high-resistance situation; Create visual choice boards; Implement choices proactively before resistance occurs; Honor the student's choice immediately; Expand the range and complexity of choices over time; Teach the student to self-advocate for choices
resourcesNeeded: Visual choice boards with Velcro-attached options; Menu of acceptable choices for each activity/transition (for teacher reference); Timer for choice-making (some students need a defined time to decide; offer 10-15 seconds to prevent anxiety); Tip: Both choices must be genuine; if one option is clearly inferior, the student will perceive manipulation; Tip: For students who are overwhelmed by choices, start with just two options and never exceed three until the student demonstrates comfort with decision-making
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student refuses all offered choices*: Add a "Your Idea" option where the student can propose an alternative that must be approved by the teacher. If the student still refuses, calmly state that you will choose for them this time, and they can choose next time. Avoid power struggles. - *Student al
caseStudySummary: Ben, an 11-year-old fifth grader with ASD and ADHD, refused to begin work an average of 8 times per day, resulting in a daily average of 45 minutes lost to refusal episodes. His teacher implemented structured choice-making across the day: (1) morning work: choice of pencil or computer, (2) math: cho
successCriteriaSummary: Task refusal episodes decrease by at least 60% within 6 weeks; Student makes a selection from offered choices within 15 seconds at least 80% of the time; Student independently requests choices or expresses preferences at least 2 times per day; Data source: Refusal episode frequency log, choice-making response time tracking, and self-advocacy tally recorded daily

functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation (distress during transitions, elopement, refusal to change activities); Communication (difficulty processing verbal transition warnings); Learning Skills (loss of instructional time due to prolonged transitions)
briefOverview: A transition object and ritual protocol provides the student with a consistent, predictable physical object or brief routine that bridges the gap between one activity and the next, reducing the anxiety and behavioral disruption that transitions commonly cause for students with autism. evidenceBaseSummary: Moreno and O'Neal address transitions as one of the most universally challenging aspects of the school day for students with ASD. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the most problematic transitions; Select a transition object; Design the transition ritual; Create a visual representation of the ritual; Teach the ritual using modeling and prompting; Implement consistently across all targeted transitions; Fade the transition object when the ritual itself becomes sufficient
resourcesNeeded: Transition object(s) selected with student input; Visual sequence card for the transition ritual; Visual timer for advance warnings; Designated spots for the transition object in each environment (hook, box, shelf); Tip: Give the transition a purposeful role: "You need to deliver this attendance folder to the office" makes the student a helper, not a patient
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student becomes rigid about the transition object and melts down if it is unavailable*: Have a backup transition object. Periodically practice transitions without the object in a low-stakes context. Teach the student that the ritual (the steps) is the anchor, not the specific object. - *Student t
caseStudySummary: Emma, a 6-year-old kindergartener with ASD and anxiety, cried and dropped to the floor during 7 out of 8 daily transitions, requiring physical prompts and an average of 8 minutes per transition to move to the next activity. The team implemented a transition ritual: Emma received a 2-minute warning v
successCriteriaSummary: Student completes the transition ritual independently (without prompting) in 80% of transitions within 6 weeks; Transition-related behavioral incidents decrease by at least 70% from baseline; Average transition time decreases to within 1 minute of the class average; Data source: Transition data log recording prompt level, behavioral incidents, and time per transition, completed daily by classroom staff

functionalNeedAreas: Communication (difficulty self-reporting about their day, needs, or experiences); Behavior Regulation (behaviors that fluctuate based on home or school variables); Learning Skills (need for consistent skill practice across environments)
briefOverview: A home-school communication consistency system establishes structured, predictable, and bidirectional information sharing between the student's home and school environments to ensure that strategies, behavioral expectations, routines, and emotional support are consistent across settings. For student
evidenceBaseSummary: Moreno and O'Neal emphasize home-school collaboration as a non-negotiable component of effective autism programming. Iovannone, Dunlap, Huber, and Kincaid (2003) identified family-professional collaboration as one of the six core components of effective educational programming for students with ASD.
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Design the communication tool; Establish the communication protocol; Implement the daily exchange; Embed key information into daily planning; Use the communication tool to coordinate strategies; Review patterns monthly; Include the student in the process age-appropriately
resourcesNeeded: Daily communication form/template (paper or digital); Dedicated folder or binder for the physical form that travels daily; Digital platform (ClassDojo, Seesaw, Google Forms, or a simple shared document) for convenience; Mutual commitment from home and school teams; Tip: Keep the form brief (5 minutes maximum to complete); if it is burdensome, it will not be maintained
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Parent does not consistently fill out the form*: Simplify the parent section to essential information only (e.g., sleep: good/ok/bad; morning: calm/upset; anything to know: ___). Call or text parents who stop responding to troubleshoot barriers. Emphasize that even minimal information is valuable

successCriteriaSummary: Communication tool is completed by both home and school at least 80% of school days; At least 2 actionable patterns are identified from communication data within the first month; At least 1 strategy is aligned across home and school environments within the first 6 weeks; Data source: Communication tool completion tracking, monthly pattern review documentation, and parent/teacher satisfaction survey at the quarterly IEP progress review

functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation (specific target behaviors the student needs to learn or increase); Communication (difficulty remembering social expectations in the moment); Learning Skills (need for motivational support to engage with behavioral expectations)
strategyName: Power Card Strategy for Motivational Behavioral Support
briefOverview: The Power Card strategy uses a student's special interest, hero, or preferred character as a motivational framework to teach and reinforce target behaviors. A brief scenario is written in which the student's hero encounters the same challenging situation the student faces and successfully uses the t
evidenceBaseSummary: The Power Card Strategy was developed by Elisa Gagnon (2001) and is described by Moreno and O'Neal as a highly practical tool for students with ASD. Gagnon's original work provided case studies and implementation guidelines. Keeling, Myles, Gagnon, and Simpson (2003) conducted a study demonstrating
applicableDiagnoses: Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety

functionalNeedAreas: Social Communication; Emotional Regulation; Joint Attention and Engagement
briefOverview: Joint Attention Scaffolding involves the systematic use of adult-mediated prompts and environmental arrangements to help a student initiate and respond to bids for shared attention with peers and adults. The teacher positions themselves at the student's eye level, follows the student's focus of inte
evidenceBaseSummary: The SCERTS Model (Social Communication, Emotional Regulation, and Transactional Support) is grounded in over two decades of developmental research on autism. applicableDiagnoses: Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Baseline Observation; Environmental Arrangement; Follow the Student's Lead; Model Shared Referencing; Prompt Hierarchy for Initiation; Reinforce and Expand; Data Collection and Review
resourcesNeeded: SCERTS Assessment Process (SAP) forms from Volume II of the SCERTS manual; Motivating objects and toys specific to student interests; Visual supports (e.g., "look" card with eyes icon) for students who benefit from visual prompts; Paraprofessional or related service provider to support data collection; Tip: For students at the Social Partner stage, focus on simple gaze shifts; for those at the Language Partner stage, incorporate verbal commenting

successCriteriaSummary: Student initiates joint attention (pointing, showing, or gaze shifting to share interest) at least 5 times per 15-minute observation period across 3 consecutive sessions; Student responds to adult or peer joint attention bids within 5 seconds at least 80% of opportunities across 3 consecutive data collection days; Student demonstrates joint attention skills in at least 2 different settings or with 2 different partners; Data source: Frequency tally sheets completed during embedded sessions, reviewed weekly using the SCERTS Assessment Process (SAP)

functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation; Self-Regulation; Social Communication

applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

resourcesNeeded: SCERTS Assessment Process (SAP) Emotional Regulation forms; One-page visual plan template (laminated for durability); Sensory tools identified as effective for the individual student (e.g., noise-canceling headphones, fidget tools, weighted lap pad); Quiet area or calm-down space in the classroom; Tip: Include the student in identifying what helps them calm down whenever developmentally appropriate

successCriteriaSummary: Reduction in dysregulation episodes from baseline by at least 50% within 6 weeks; Average duration of dysregulation episodes decreases by at least 50% from baseline within 6 weeks; Student begins to use at least one self-initiated regulation strategy (demonstrating transition from mutual to self-regulation) within 10 weeks; Data source: Daily frequency and duration logs maintained by classroom staff, reviewed weekly using the SCERTS Emotional Regulation tracking form

functionalNeedAreas: Social Communication; Emotional Regulation; Executive Function; Transitions and Routine Participation
briefOverview: Transactional Support Mapping is a proactive planning process in which the IEP team systematically analyzes every major classroom routine and transition, identifies the specific interpersonal and environmental supports the student needs to participate successfully, and then embeds those supports dir
evidenceBaseSummary: Prizant et al. (2006) describe Transactional Support as one of the three core domains of the SCERTS Model, detailed extensively in Volume II, Chapters 7-9. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

resourcesNeeded: SCERTS Transactional Support Planning forms (from Volume II); Visual schedule materials (pictures, icons, written schedules based on student level); Timer or visual timer for transition warnings; Matrix template (can be created in a spreadsheet or table format); Tip: Start by mapping the 3 most challenging routines first, then expand to the full day
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *The map is too complex for staff to reference quickly*: Create a simplified "cheat sheet" version with icons and color coding that can be attached to a clipboard or lanyard. Reserve the detailed version for team meetings. - *Supports are implemented in the self-contained setting but not in genera

successCriteriaSummary: Student participates in at least 80% of daily routines with no more than minimal support within 8 weeks; Reduction of routine-specific challenging behaviors by at least 60% from baseline within 6 weeks; Transactional Support Map is implemented with at least 85% fidelity as measured by weekly fidelity checks; Data source: SCERTS participation rating scale completed weekly across all routines, fidelity checklist completed by an observer weekly

functionalNeedAreas: Social Communication; Expressive Language; Pragmatic Language

applicableDiagnoses: Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

resourcesNeeded: Clear sealed containers, ziplock bags, or locked boxes; Preferred items and activities identified through preference assessment; AAC device or picture exchange system if applicable; Data collection form or app for tracking communication attempts; Tip: Rotate temptation types to maintain novelty and prevent the student from habituating to one setup
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student becomes frustrated and has a behavioral response instead of communicating*: Reduce the difficulty of the temptation by making the desired item partially visible or offering a model prompt immediately. Gradually increase the wait time as the student learns the routine. - *Staff inadvertent

He had an AAC device with 30 symbols but rarely used it spontaneously. His speech-language pathologist and teacher embedded 6 communicatio
successCriteriaSummary: Student spontaneously communicates (without prompting) in at least 80% of communication temptation opportunities across 3 consecutive data days; Student uses communication for at least 3 different functions (requesting, protesting, commenting) within a single school day; Student generalizes spontaneous communication to at least 2 non-temptation contexts within 8 weeks; Data source: Communication temptation data log tracking opportunity, student response, communication form, and prompt level, reviewed weekly

functionalNeedAreas: Social Communication; Expressive Language; Pragmatic Language; Language Processing
strategyName: Natural Language Sampling and Goal Embedding
briefOverview: Natural Language Sampling and Goal Embedding is a strategy in which the teacher and speech-language pathologist systematically collect samples of the student's spontaneous language during natural activities, analyze patterns in the student's communication (including unconventional forms like echolal
evidenceBaseSummary: Prizant and colleagues have published extensively on the functional nature of echolalia and unconventional verbal behavior in ASD (Prizant, 1983; Prizant & Duchan, 1981; Prizant & Rydell, 1984). applicableDiagnoses: Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Collect Natural Language Samples; Analyze the Samples; Identify Functional Goals; Map Goals to Activities; Implement Embedded Intervention; Re-sample Monthly; Share with the Team
resourcesNeeded: Audio or video recording device (smartphone or tablet is sufficient); Language sample transcription template or software (e.g., SALT software); SCERTS Communication Stage reference charts (from Volume II); Tip: If the student uses echolalia, do not dismiss it. Analyze the context to understand the communicative intent (e.g., a student who says "Time for juice?" when thirsty is using delayed echolalia to request); Tip: Involve parents by collecting a home language sample once per quarter to compare communication across settings
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student is minimally verbal and the sample yields few communicative acts*: Extend the sampling period and include pre-intentional behaviors (e.g., reaching, body orientation, facial expressions) in the analysis. Use the SCERTS Social Partner framework to set appropriate pre-verbal goals. - *Team
caseStudySummary: Sophia, a 6-year-old first grader with Autism Spectrum Disorder, used a mix of single words, delayed echolalia (e.g., repeating lines from favorite TV shows), and a 20-symbol AAC board. successCriteriaSummary: Monthly natural language samples show a measurable increase in the number and diversity of spontaneous communicative acts compared to baseline; Student achieves at least 2 of 3 embedded communication goals within 12 weeks, as measured by activity-based data collection; Student demonstrates target communication forms in at least 2 activities or settings not specifically targeted in intervention; Data source: Monthly natural language sample analysis (transcription with coded communicative acts), activity-based goal tracking data collected 3 times per week

bookTitle: Visual Supports for People with Autism
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function; Independence in Daily Routines; Communication (receptive); Transition Management
briefOverview: A Visual Activity Schedule is a set of photographs, icons, or written text arranged in sequence that shows a student exactly what activities they will do, in what order, and what comes next. evidenceBaseSummary: Cohen and Gerhardt (2013) dedicate Chapter 4 of their book to visual activity schedules, drawing heavily on the research of McClannahan and Krantz (1999, 2010) at the Princeton Child Development Institute, who demonstrated that photographic activity schedules enabled children with ASD to complete co
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Assess Student's Visual Symbol Level; Select the Schedule Format; Create the Schedule Materials; Teach Schedule Following with Physical Prompting; Fade Prompts Systematically; Expand the Schedule; Generalize Across Settings
resourcesNeeded: Small binder, clipboard, or tablet with schedule app (e.g., First Then Visual Schedule, Choiceworks); Laminated photos or icons with Velcro; "Finished" envelope or pocket for completed items; Tip: Always prompt from behind the student, never from the front, to avoid creating dependence on adult facial cues; Tip: Include at least one highly preferred activity on each schedule to maintain motivation
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student becomes prompt-dependent and waits for adult cue before checking schedule*: Ensure prompts are delivered from behind and faded systematically. If prompt dependency develops, introduce a time delay (wait 10 seconds before prompting) and gradually increase the delay. - *Student skips activi

His IEP team introduced a photographic activity schedule in
successCriteriaSummary: Student independently initiates checking the schedule without adult prompting in at least 90% of transitions across 5 consecutive school days; Student completes all scheduled activities in the correct sequence with 0 adult prompts for at least 3 consecutive days; Verbal and physical prompts from adults decrease by at least 80% from baseline within 6 weeks; Data source: Prompt level tracking sheet completed at each transition, recording whether the student initiated independently, needed a gestural prompt, or needed a physical prompt

bookTitle: Visual Supports for People with Autism
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function; Sequential Processing; Task Completion and Independence; Visual Processing
briefOverview: A Visual Task Analysis Strip is a portable, sequenced set of images or text that breaks a single multi-step task into its component steps, presented in a linear strip or card format that the student references while completing the task. Unlike a daily schedule (which shows which activities to do), a
evidenceBaseSummary: Cohen and Gerhardt (2013) address visual task analysis in Chapter 5, describing it as one of the most versatile visual support tools. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Select the Target Task; Perform a Detailed Task Analysis; Verify the Analysis with the Student; Create the Visual Strip; Teach Using the Strip; Fade Prompts and Monitor; Generalize and Maintain
resourcesNeeded: Laminated strip or flip card set with photographs or icons; Velcro check-off markers or dry-erase checkboxes; Mounting options: Velcro strip on wall near task location, keyring flip cards, or clipboard; Tip: Post the strip at the point of performance (e.g., handwashing strip mounted near the sink); Tip: Use actual photographs of the student's environment (their classroom sink, their desk) for maximum clarity
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student ignores the strip and attempts the task from memory, missing steps*: Introduce a physical interaction requirement, such as flipping each card or placing a check mark, so that engagement with the strip is built into the routine. - *Task has too many steps and the student is overwhelmed*: B
caseStudySummary: Olivia, a 12-year-old sixth grader with Autism Spectrum Disorder and ADHD, could not independently complete her end-of-day packing routine, which required her to (1) check her assignment notebook, (2) gather homework materials from her desk, (3) place them in her backpack, (4) check her locker for a
successCriteriaSummary: Student independently completes 100% of steps in the task analysis for 5 consecutive days without prompting; Errors or omitted steps decrease from baseline to 0 within 4 weeks; Student independently initiates use of the visual strip (picks it up, references it) without adult prompting at least 90% of opportunities; Data source: Step-completion checklist where staff record which steps were completed independently, with prompting, or omitted, collected 3 times per week

bookTitle: Visual Supports for People with Autism
functionalNeedAreas: Executive Function; Visual Processing; Attention and On-Task Behavior; Spatial Organization; Independence in Learning
strategyName: Visual Boundary and Work System Design
briefOverview: Visual Boundary and Work System Design involves structuring the physical classroom environment with clear visual markers that communicate where specific activities happen, where materials belong, what work needs to be done, and how the student will know when they are finished. Drawing on the TEACCH
evidenceBaseSummary: Cohen and Gerhardt (2013) discuss environmental structuring in Chapters 2 and 3, drawing extensively on Schopler, Mesibov, and Hearsey's (1995) TEACCH Structured Teaching framework. Hume and Odom (2007) demonstrated that individual work systems increased on-task behavior and decreased teacher prompt
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Audit the Current Environment; Define Activity Areas; Install Visual Boundaries; Set Up Individual Work Systems; Label All Materials and Storage; Teach the Student to Use the System; Maintain and Adjust
resourcesNeeded: Colored floor tape (multiple colors); Label maker or printed labels with icons and text; Bins, baskets, or trays for "To Do" and "Finished" areas; Room dividers or bookshelves for physical boundaries (optional); Visual labels from icon libraries (e.g., Boardmaker, LessonPix)
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *The general education teacher is resistant to modifying the classroom layout*: Start with a small, portable work system at the student's desk rather than changing the whole room. Demonstrate its effectiveness over 2-3 weeks, then discuss whether broader environmental modifications might benefit a
caseStudySummary: Isaiah, an 8-year-old third grader with Autism Spectrum Disorder, spent approximately 40% of independent work time off-task, wandering the classroom, sorting through materials bins, or sitting at his desk without beginning work. successCriteriaSummary: Student's on-task behavior during independent work periods increases to at least 85% as measured by momentary time sampling (30-second intervals); Student independently retrieves and returns materials to labeled locations at least 90% of the time without adult prompting; Wandering or off-task behavior decreases by at least 70% from baseline within 4 weeks; Data source: Momentary time sampling data collected during 2 independent work periods per day, 3 days per week; material retrieval/return tracking by classroom staff

bookTitle: Visual Supports for People with Autism
functionalNeedAreas: Communication (Expressive); Self-Determination and Agency; Executive Function; Behavior Regulation
briefOverview: A Visual Choice Board System is a structured display of two or more options represented by photographs, icons, or text that allows a student to independently make selections across various classroom activities, including choosing preferred activities, selecting materials, indicating food preferences
evidenceBaseSummary: Cohen and Gerhardt (2013) dedicate a section of Chapter 6 to choice-making visual supports, emphasizing their role in both communication development and behavior reduction. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Conduct a Preference Assessment; Identify Choice Opportunities; Create Choice Boards; Teach Choice-Making; Honor All Choices Immediately; Expand Complexity; Fade to Natural Choice-Making
resourcesNeeded: Laminated choice boards (multiple boards for different contexts or one universal board); Velcro-backed icon set organized by category; Icon library (Boardmaker, LessonPix, or photographs); Preference assessment data form; Tip: Rotate options regularly to maintain interest and prevent selection of the same item out of habit rather than true preference

Rotate the position of options and conduct a brief preference re-assessment. If the student genuinely prefers one option, respect that while gradua

Her team hypothesized that the behaviors were partially related to a lack of cont
successCriteriaSummary: Student independently selects from the choice board (without prompting) in at least 90% of offered choice opportunities across 5 consecutive days; Challenging behavior at choice/transition points decreases by at least 60% from baseline within 4 weeks; Student demonstrates choice-making in at least 2 contexts beyond those with formal choice boards within 8 weeks; Data source: Choice board use log (recording whether the student initiated, was prompted, or refused to use the board at each opportunity), paired with a frequency count of challenging behavior at transition points

bookTitle: Visual Supports for People with Autism
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Cause-and-Effect Understanding; Executive Function (Planning and Decision-Making); Communication (Understanding Expectations)
briefOverview: A Visual Contingency Map is a flowchart-style visual support that explicitly shows the student the relationship between their behavior choices and the consequences that follow. resourcesNeeded: Laminated contingency map (one per target situation); Icons or photographs representing the situation, behaviors, and consequences; Green and red border markers or colored paper for positive and less preferred pathways; Tip: Keep the map simple; avoid more than two branching pathways initially; Tip: Never use the map as a threat ("Look at what happens if you don't behave"). successCriteriaSummary: Expected behavior occurs in at least 80% of target situations across 5 consecutive days within 4 weeks; Challenging behavior decreases by at least 70% from baseline within 4 weeks; Student independently references or mentions the contingency map without adult prompting at least once per day within 6 weeks; Data source: ABC (Antecedent-Behavior-Consequence) data collected during every target situation, recording which behavioral pathway occurred and whether the map was reviewed proactively

bookTitle: Social Skills Training for Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders

briefOverview: Structured Peer Mediated Intervention involves systematically training typically developing peers to initiate social interactions, provide prompts, and reinforce social engagement in students with Autism Spectrum Disorder during structured and unstructured school activities. Rather than relying sole

applicableDiagnoses: Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Select Peer Partners; Conduct Peer Training; Arrange Structured Social Opportunities; Facilitate Initial Sessions; Fade Adult Support; Conduct Peer Booster Sessions; Monitor and Expand
resourcesNeeded: Peer training curriculum (Baker provides examples in his book; additional resources include the Peer Networks model by Haring & Breen); Structured activities and games appropriate for the age group; Parental consent forms for peer participants; Adult facilitator (teacher, paraprofessional, school psychologist, or social worker) for training and initial sessions; Tip: Rotate peer partners to prevent fatigue and to expose the focus student to different social styles

successCriteriaSummary: Student engages in at least 3 reciprocal social interactions with peers per day across 5 consecutive school days (up from baseline); Duration of social engagement per interaction increases by at least 100% from baseline within 6 weeks; Student initiates at least 1 social interaction per day without adult prompting within 8 weeks; Data source: Peer interaction observation log completed during structured social times, recording frequency, duration, initiator, and quality (parallel, cooperative, or reciprocal) of interactions

bookTitle: Social Skills Training for Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders
functionalNeedAreas: Social Skills; Communication (Pragmatic); Self-Confidence in Social Situations; Behavioral Flexibility
briefOverview: Behavioral Rehearsal with Video Self-Modeling combines two evidence-based techniques: the student practices a target social skill through structured role-play (behavioral rehearsal) and then watches a carefully edited video of themselves performing the skill correctly (video self-modeling). evidenceBaseSummary: Tomlinson introduces Think-Tac-Toe in Chapter 9 as a practical tool for differentiating by interest and learning profile. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; OCD

author: Jessica Minahan & Nancy Rappaport

resourcesNeeded: Environmental scan checklist (available in The Behavior Code companion materials); Flexible seating options (study carrel, wobble cushion, alternative desk placement); Noise-reducing headphones or earplugs; Timer for transition warnings; Tip: Make one change at a time when possible so you can isolate which modification has the greatest impact

applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Anxiety; Depression

successCriteriaSummary: Student-teacher rating concordance reaches 75% or higher within 4 weeks; Target behavior frequency improves by at least 50% within 6 weeks; Student independently uses the self-monitoring form without prompting for at least 2 consecutive weeks; Data source: Self-monitoring rating forms; teacher rating comparison logs; weekly point total graphs

author: Jessica Minahan & Nancy Rappaport
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Emotional Regulation
briefOverview: The 2x10 strategy involves a teacher spending 2 minutes per day for 10 consecutive days engaging in personal, non-academic conversation with a student who exhibits challenging behavior. The conversation must be about topics the student cares about and must have no connection to academics, behavior e
evidenceBaseSummary: Minahan and Rappaport stress the primacy of the student-teacher relationship throughout The Behavior Code, particularly in Chapters 1 and 2, drawing on attachment theory (Bowlby, 1969) and the research of Pianta (1999) on student-teacher relationships as protective factors. The 2x10 approach has bee
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the student; Research the student's interests; Schedule the 2 minutes daily; Engage in genuine conversation for 2 minutes; Maintain the practice beyond 10 days; Track behavioral and engagement changes
resourcesNeeded: No materials needed beyond time and intentionality; Student interest inventory (optional, for initial planning); Tip: The 2 minutes must feel authentic. Do not treat it as a compliance strategy in front of the student. Genuine curiosity is essential.; Tip: If a student is initially resistant to conversation, sit nearby and make brief comments without requiring responses. Proximity and consistency build trust.; Adaptation for secondary teachers with many students: Identify 2-3 highest-need students and rotate 2x10 cycles throughout the semester.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student is unresponsive or gives one-word answers*: Continue showing up consistently. Malik frequently argued with Mr. Torres, refused work, and had been suspended twice. Mr. Torres learned that Malik was passi
successCriteriaSummary: Student initiates positive interaction with the teacher at least once per week by the end of the 10-day cycle; Compliance with teacher requests improves by at least 30% within 4 weeks; Office discipline referrals decrease by at least 50% within one grading period; Data source: Teacher interaction log; office referral data; compliance tally during direct instruction periods

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation; Behavior Regulation
strategyName: Drilling to Identify Specific Unsolved Problems
briefOverview: "Drilling" is a conversational technique used during the Empathy step of Collaborative & Proactive Solutions (CPS) to help adults identify the specific, concrete concern a child has about a particular unsolved problem. Rather than accepting a child's initial vague or surface-level response ("I don't
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene dedicates extensive attention to drilling in Chapters 7 and 8 of The Explosive Child (3rd edition), positioning it as the skill that most distinguishes effective from ineffective CPS implementation. The technique draws on motivational interviewing principles (Miller & Rollnick, 2002), particu
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the unsolved problem; Open the Empathy step; Receive the initial response without judgment; Apply drilling strategies; Confirm the specific concern; Document the concern for problem-solving; Practice regularly
resourcesNeeded: Quiet, private location for the conversation; Unsolved problems list (written in advance); Drilling strategy reference card for the adult (laminated prompt card with the four strategies); Tip: Never argue with the student's stated concern, even if it seems illogical. Understanding their perspective is the goal, not correcting it.; Adaptation for students with limited verbal skills: Use visual supports, choice boards, or picture cards to help the student indicate their concern.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student repeatedly says "I don't know"*: This is common and does not mean the child is being defiant. caseStudySummary: Ms. Chen, a 3rd-grade teacher, had an unsolved problem: "Brianna has difficulty starting her writing assignment during journal time." When Ms. Chen asked, "What's up?" successCriteriaSummary: Adult accurately identifies the student's specific concern (verified by student confirmation) in at least 80% of drilling attempts within 4 weeks of practice; Time needed to reach the specific concern decreases from 10+ minutes to under 5 minutes within 6 weeks; Solutions generated after effective drilling have a durability rate of at least 70% (i.e., they last 2+ weeks without needing revision); Data source: CPS conversation logs with documentation of concerns identified and solution durability tracking

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation; Behavior Regulation
strategyName: Lagging Skills Identification and Targeted Skill-Building
briefOverview: Greene's model posits that challenging behavior occurs when the demands of the environment exceed a child's capacity to respond adaptively, and this capacity gap is due to specific lagging cognitive skills such as flexibility, frustration tolerance, problem-solving, or shifting from one mindset to a
evidenceBaseSummary: The lagging skills framework is central to The Explosive Child and is detailed in Chapters 3-5. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Review the lagging skills checklist; Cross-reference lagging skills with unsolved problems; Prioritize 2-3 lagging skills for focus; Engage the student in CPS conversations; Scaffold the skill during the conversation; Track skill development
resourcesNeeded: Lagging Skills and Unsolved Problems checklist (free at livesinthebalance.org); Quiet space for CPS conversations; Documentation log for tracking which skills are exercised in each conversation; Tip: Do not try to "teach" lagging skills in a pull-out social skills group. Greene emphasizes that skills develop through real-world practice within authentic problem-solving contexts.; Adaptation for very young children (K-1): Simplify language and use puppets or visual scenarios to scaffold the problem-solving conversation.

Prioritize the unsolved problems that are most disruptive, and the associated skil
caseStudySummary: Theo, a 5th-grade student with autism spectrum disorder and ADHD, had 14 lagging skills checked on Greene's inventory, including difficulty managing emotional response to frustration, difficulty shifting from one task to another, and difficulty considering a range of solutions to a problem. The team
successCriteriaSummary: At least 2 lagging skills show measurable improvement on the checklist within 8-10 weeks; Student participation in CPS conversations increases in quality (e.g., student generates at least one solution independently within 6 weeks); Behavioral incidents associated with the prioritized lagging skills decrease by at least 40%; Data source: Lagging Skills and Unsolved Problems checklist rated every 4-6 weeks; CPS conversation quality log; behavioral incident frequency data

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation; Behavior Regulation
briefOverview: The Invitation is the third and final step in Greene's Collaborative & Proactive Solutions process, in which the adult invites the child to brainstorm solutions that address both the child's concern and the adult's concern simultaneously. This is distinct from the adult proposing a solution or offer
evidenceBaseSummary: Greene outlines the Invitation step in Chapters 8-10 of The Explosive Child, positioning it as the mechanism through which durable solutions emerge. The approach aligns with self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985), which demonstrates that autonomy and competence are fundamental human needs, an
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Complete the Empathy step fully; State the adult concern clearly and concisely; Deliver the Invitation; Evaluate solutions collaboratively; Select a mutually satisfactory solution; Establish a check-in plan; Follow through on the check-in
resourcesNeeded: Private, quiet space free from distractions; Written record of the agreed-upon solution (can be a simple post-it or a formal document); Tip: If the student cannot generate any ideas, offer two tentative suggestions and ask which seems closer to workable, or whether neither works and why; Tip: Solutions do not need to be perfect. They need to be "good enough" to try.; Adaptation for younger students: Use concrete visual aids (e.g., draw the two concerns as two circles and the solution as where they overlap)
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student proposes unrealistic solutions*: Rather than dismissing them, test them against the two concerns. "That would definitely help with your concern. I'm wondering though, how would it help with my concern about [X]?" This teaches the student to evaluate solutions, a critical thinking skill. -
caseStudySummary: Olivia, an 8th-grade student with anxiety and depression, frequently skipped her first-period science class by going to the counselor's office. Through drilling, her teacher learned Olivia's concern was that the class started with a rapid-fire quiz review that made her feel panicked and unprepared.
successCriteriaSummary: Student and adult successfully agree on a mutually satisfactory solution in at least 75% of CPS conversations within 6 weeks; Agreed-upon solutions remain durable (no recurrence of the unsolved problem) for at least 2 weeks in at least 60% of cases; Student generates at least one solution idea independently in CPS conversations within 4 weeks; Data source: CPS conversation log documenting solutions agreed upon and follow-up outcomes; durability tracking form

author: Ross Greene
functionalNeedAreas: Emotional Regulation; Behavior Regulation
briefOverview: While Greene's preferred approach is Proactive Plan B (solving problems in advance during calm moments), Emergency Plan B is a protocol for using collaborative problem-solving principles during an active behavioral escalation when a child is frustrated, upset, or on the verge of a meltdown. evidenceBaseSummary: Greene discusses Emergency Plan B in Chapter 9 of The Explosive Child, distinguishing it from Proactive Plan B and explaining its appropriate use. applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; OCD

resourcesNeeded: Designated calming space in or near the classroom; De-escalation script card for the teacher (laminated, in a pocket or on the desk); Clear school protocol for when to call for additional support; Tip: Emergency Plan B is NOT the same as ignoring behavior or "letting the student get away with it." It is a strategic delay that preserves the opportunity for durable problem-solving.; Adaptation for students who elope: Have a pre-arranged safe space and a trusted adult the student can go to during escalation, established during calm times through Proactive Plan B.

successCriteriaSummary: Duration of escalation episodes decreases by 40% within 4 weeks of consistent Emergency Plan B use; Episodes that previously required office removal are resolved in the classroom at least 60% of the time; Every Emergency Plan B episode is followed by a Proactive Plan B conversation within 48 hours (tracked at 90% fidelity); Data source: Incident duration logs; office removal data; Proactive Plan B follow-up tracking form

author: Ross Greene

strategyName: Surrogate Problem-Solving Process for Nonverbal or Reluctant Students

successCriteriaSummary: Team correctly identifies the student's primary concern (as evidenced by behavioral improvement following matched intervention) within 2-3 hypothesis cycles; Target behavior associated with the unsolved problem decreases by at least 50% within 6 weeks; Surrogate process gradually transitions to supported direct student participation (if developmentally appropriate) within one academic year; Data source: Hypothesis-testing log with behavioral outcome data; frequency data on target behaviors; team meeting notes documenting hypothesis revisions

author: Glen Dunlap et al.
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Instructional Access

briefOverview: The PTR-YC model provides a structured, team-based functional behavior assessment (FBA) process specifically designed for children ages 2-6 in early childhood settings. The PTR-YC team (teacher, teaching assistant, Marcus's grandmother, and the program's behavior
successCriteriaSummary: Target behavior rating on the PTR-YC scale improves by at least 2 points (on the 5-point scale) within 4-6 weeks; Desired replacement behavior rating on the PTR-YC scale improves by at least 2 points within 4-6 weeks; Team completes daily ratings with at least 80% fidelity over the monitoring period; Data source: PTR-YC daily behavior rating scale; weekly graphs; team meeting notes

author: Glen Dunlap et al.
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Instructional Access

author: Glen Dunlap et al.
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Instructional Access
briefOverview: The "Teach" component of PTR-YC involves explicitly and systematically teaching a young child a specific replacement behavior that serves the same function as the challenging behavior but is socially appropriate. If a child screams to get attention, the replacement behavior might be tapping the teac
evidenceBaseSummary: Chapter 6 of PTR-YC details the Teach strategies, grounded in the functional communication training (FCT) research of Carr and Durand (1985), which is one of the most well-validated interventions in the behavioral literature. Tiger et al. (2008) published a comprehensive review of FCT across 100+ st
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability

resourcesNeeded: Picture cards or communication supports specific to the replacement behavior; AAC device (if applicable); Reinforcement items matched to the behavior's function; Timer for break requests (to ensure the child returns to the activity); Tip: In the early stages, ALWAYS honor the replacement behavior immediately. A child who requests a break and is told "not right now" will learn that the replacement behavior does not work and will return to the challenging behavior.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Child uses the replacement behavior excessively* (e.g., requests a break every 2 minutes): This is a sign that the strategy is working — the child trusts the replacement. Gradually increase the demand before providing the reinforcement (e.g., "Let's do one more, then you can have a break") while
caseStudySummary: Elijah, a 4-year-old with autism spectrum disorder in an inclusive pre-K classroom, bit other children to access preferred toys (tangible function), averaging 3 biting incidents per week. The team taught Elijah to hand a picture card showing "my turn" to a peer or teacher when he wanted a toy. Teach
successCriteriaSummary: Child uses the replacement behavior independently (without prompting) at least 3 times per day within 4 weeks; Challenging behavior decreases by at least 70% within 6 weeks; Prompting level fades from physical/verbal to natural cue or independent within 4-6 weeks; Data source: Replacement behavior frequency count (prompted vs. independent); challenging behavior frequency count; prompt level tracking

author: Glen Dunlap et al.
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Instructional Access
briefOverview: The "Reinforce" component of the PTR-YC model focuses on designing consequence strategies that strengthen desirable behavior while reducing the payoff for challenging behavior. This involves two simultaneous actions: (1) delivering meaningful, function-matched reinforcement whenever the child engage
evidenceBaseSummary: Chapter 7 of PTR-YC details the Reinforce strategies, building on the foundational reinforcement research of Skinner (1953) and the applied work of Baer, Wolf, and Risley (1968). The combination of reinforcement for desired behavior with extinction of challenging behavior is supported by extensive s
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability
implementationStepsSummary: Identify the reinforcement that matches the behavior's function; Conduct a reinforcement preference assessment; Design the reinforcement schedule; Plan the extinction component; Implement a positive interaction strategy; Deliver reinforcement with immediacy, specificity, and enthusiasm; Thin the reinforcement schedule gradually
resourcesNeeded: Reinforcement preference assessment results (list of 5-8 items/activities); Self-monitoring tool for tracking positive interaction ratio (paperclip method, tally counter, or app); Visual cue for staff reminding them to "catch the child being good"; Timer or reminder for scheduled reinforcement delivery; Tip: Reinforcement must be experienced as reinforcing by the CHILD, not the adult. If verbal praise does not change the child's behavior, it is not functioning as a reinforcer for that child, regardless of the adult's intent.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Extinction burst — behavior gets worse before it gets better*: Prepare the team in advance. An extinction burst is actually a sign that the intervention is working (the child is noticing that the old behavior no longer produces the expected outcome). Maintain fidelity for at least 2-3 weeks. - *O
caseStudySummary: Rosie, a 5-year-old with ADHD in a kindergarten classroom, engaged in loud, disruptive calling out during instruction (attention function), averaging 15 instances per 20-minute lesson. The team implemented: reinforcement for hand-raising (the teacher immediately called on Rosie when her hand went up
successCriteriaSummary: Desired behavior frequency increases by at least 100% (doubles) within 4 weeks; Challenging behavior frequency decreases by at least 50% within 6 weeks; Positive-to-negative interaction ratio reaches at least 4:1 consistently (measured by weekly self-monitoring); Data source: Behavior frequency tallies during target routines; positive interaction ratio self-monitoring tool; sticker chart data

author: Glen Dunlap et al.
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; Instructional Access
briefOverview: The PTR-YC model places family participation at the center of the intervention process, requiring that at least one family member serve as a full member of the intervention team from assessment through monitoring. This strategy involves actively involving the family in identifying the challenging be
evidenceBaseSummary: Dunlap et al. emphasize family involvement throughout the PTR-YC manual, drawing on the family systems research of Turnbull and Turnbull (2001) and the family-centered practices framework in early intervention (Dunst et al., 2002). Research consistently demonstrates that family involvement in early
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Invite the family as full team members; Gather family perspective during assessment; Include the family in hypothesis development; Collaboratively select intervention strategies; Develop a home component; Establish a communication system; Check in with the family regularly
resourcesNeeded: PTR-YC family interview protocol (from the manual); Simple, visual home strategy guide (1-page handout with pictures); Daily communication tool (notebook, app like ClassDojo, or text message template); Translated materials for families whose home language is not English; Tip: Respect the family's capacity. If a family is dealing with housing instability, health challenges, or other stressors, asking them to implement complex behavior strategies at home may not be appropriate. Focus on the school component and ask the family to contribute in ways that feel manageable.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Family is unable or unwilling to attend meetings*: Offer alternative formats (phone calls, home visits, video calls). Send home the assessment checklists with simple instructions and collect their input in writing. - *Cultural differences in perspectives on discipline*: Approach conversations wit
caseStudySummary: Aiden, a 3-year-old with a developmental delay and emerging ADHD symptoms, exhibited frequent tantrums (dropping to the floor, screaming) during transitions at both preschool and home, averaging 5 per day at school and 4 per day at home. Aiden's mother, Ms. Rivera, joined the PTR-YC team. During the
successCriteriaSummary: Family participates in at least 75% of team meetings (in any format) over the intervention period; At least one strategy is implemented consistently at home (family self-report and checklist confirmation); Challenging behavior decreases in both school and home settings by at least 40% within 6 weeks; Data source: Family participation log; home strategy implementation checklist (completed biweekly by family); school behavior rating scale; family-reported home behavior frequency

bookTitle: Positive Behavior Support at the School Level
author: Deanne Crone & Robert Horner
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; School Systems
briefOverview: A behavioral expectations teaching matrix is a grid that defines 3-5 school-wide expectations (such as "Be Safe," "Be Respectful," "Be Responsible") and specifies what each expectation looks like in every school setting (classroom, hallway, cafeteria, playground, restroom, bus). Rather than assuming
evidenceBaseSummary: Crone and Horner describe the teaching matrix as a foundational Tier 1 component, drawing on the school-wide PBIS research base that includes large-scale implementation studies across thousands of schools (Bradshaw et al., 2010; Horner et al., 2009). A randomized controlled trial by Bradshaw, Mitche
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Establish 3-5 positively stated school-wide expectations; Build the teaching matrix; Develop lesson plans for each matrix cell; Schedule teaching during the first 2-3 weeks of school; Reteach after breaks and as needed; Post the matrix visually; Link to the school-wide recognition system
resourcesNeeded: Poster-sized teaching matrix for each common area; Lesson plan templates for each setting; Staff training time (60-90 minutes for initial introduction plus 30-minute boosters); School-wide recognition tools (tickets, tokens, or digital points); Tip: Involve students in generating examples and non-examples during lessons; this increases engagement and ownership
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Staff view teaching expectations as "too basic" or unnecessary*: Share data showing that schools implementing explicit teaching see 20-50% reductions in office referrals. Emphasize that teaching expectations is no different from teaching reading — we do not expect students to figure it out on the
caseStudySummary: Washington Elementary, serving 450 students (42% qualifying for special education or 504 plans), adopted PBIS with a teaching matrix featuring three expectations: Be Safe, Be Kind, Be Ready. During the first three weeks of school, every class rotated through six school settings to learn expectations
successCriteriaSummary: At least 80% of students can identify and describe the school-wide expectations when asked (survey or brief assessment); Office discipline referrals decrease by at least 20% in the first year of implementation; Staff report using the expectations language consistently (at least 80% on a fidelity checklist); Data source: Student expectations knowledge survey; office discipline referral data (tracked monthly by setting); PBIS fidelity checklist (e.g., TFI — Tiered Fidelity Inventory)

bookTitle: Positive Behavior Support at the School Level
author: Deanne Crone & Robert Horner
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; School Systems
strategyName: Data-Based Decision-Making with Office Discipline Referral Analysis
briefOverview: This strategy involves systematically collecting, analyzing, and using office discipline referral (ODR) data to make informed decisions about where, when, and for whom behavioral supports need to be strengthened. Rather than reacting to individual incidents in isolation, the school leadership team r
evidenceBaseSummary: Data-based decision-making is a central pillar of the PBIS framework as described by Crone and Horner, drawing on the work of Sugai, Horner, and Todd (2000) who developed the School-Wide Information System (SWIS) for ODR data analysis. Research by Irvin et al. (2004) demonstrated that schools using
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Standardize the ODR form; Train all staff on when and how to write a referral; Enter data into a tracking system; Generate monthly data reports; Review data in monthly team meeting; Implement the data-driven action plan; Share data with the full staff
resourcesNeeded: Standardized ODR form (paper or digital); Data tracking system (SWIS, Google Sheets, or SIS); Monthly data report template with graphs; PBIS leadership team (5-7 members) with protected meeting time; Tip: The quality of the data depends on the consistency of the referral process. Invest heavily in defining and training the minor vs. major distinction.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Staff write referrals inconsistently (some over-refer, some never refer)*: Conduct calibration exercises during staff meetings using hypothetical scenarios. Review referral rates by teacher and address outliers privately. - *Data reveals racial or disability-based disparities in referrals*: This
caseStudySummary: Lincoln Middle School's PBIS team analyzed ODR data at their October meeting and discovered that 38% of all referrals occurred during the transition between 3rd period and lunch, primarily for physical contact (pushing, shoving) in the main hallway. Students with IEPs accounted for 55% of those refe
successCriteriaSummary: PBIS team meets monthly and reviews data at every meeting (100% meeting fidelity); At least one data-driven action plan is implemented per month; Overall ODR rates decrease by 20-30% year over year; Data source: Monthly ODR summary reports; team meeting minutes with action items and follow-through tracking; annual comparison reports

bookTitle: Positive Behavior Support at the School Level
author: Deanne Crone & Robert Horner
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; School Systems
briefOverview: Check-In/Check-Out (CICO) is a structured Tier 2 behavioral intervention in which a student begins each day by checking in with a designated adult, carries a daily progress report (DPR) card throughout the day on which teachers rate the student's behavior at the end of each class period, and ends th
evidenceBaseSummary: Crone and Horner describe CICO (also known as the Behavior Education Program or BEP) as the primary Tier 2 intervention in their PBIS framework, with detailed implementation guidance. The evidence base for CICO is extensive: Hawken and Horner (2003) demonstrated the intervention's effectiveness thro
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression
implementationStepsSummary: Identify students for CICO; Assign a CICO coordinator; Design the Daily Progress Report (DPR); Conduct morning check-in; Teachers rate at each period transition; Conduct afternoon check-out; Send the DPR home and review data weekly
resourcesNeeded: Daily Progress Report cards (printed in bulk); CICO coordinator (requires approximately 30-60 minutes of daily time across all students); Data tracking spreadsheet or SWIS CICO module; Small reinforcers (stickers, privileges, school store items); Parent communication (DPR serves this function)
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Student loses or destroys the DPR card*: Keep backup cards readily available and treat it matter-of-factly ("Here's a new one; let's keep going"). If card destruction is a pattern, it may indicate the student needs a function-based modification to CICO. - *Teachers forget to rate the card*: Build
caseStudySummary: Tyler, an 8th-grade student with ADHD, had accumulated 4 office discipline referrals for disruptive behavior (calling out, off-task, defiance) in the first quarter. He was placed on CICO with Mr. Davis, the school's behavioral aide, as his coordinator. Each morning, Tyler checked in with Mr. Davis,
successCriteriaSummary: Student meets the daily point goal (e.g., 80%) on at least 70% of days within 4 weeks; ODRs decrease by at least 50% during the CICO intervention period; Student demonstrates positive engagement during check-in/check-out (rated by coordinator); Data source: Daily point percentage tracking; ODR comparison data; CICO coordinator ratings

bookTitle: Positive Behavior Support at the School Level
author: Deanne Crone & Robert Horner
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; School Systems
strategyName: Tiered Fidelity Inventory (TFI) for System-Level Implementation Monitoring
briefOverview: The Tiered Fidelity Inventory is a structured assessment tool that measures how well a school is implementing PBIS across all three tiers (universal, targeted, and intensive). The TFI evaluates the presence and quality of core PBIS components — such as whether expectations are taught, whether data a
evidenceBaseSummary: The TFI was developed by Algozzine et al. (2014) as a validated tool for assessing PBIS implementation fidelity across all three tiers. Crone and Horner emphasize throughout their text that implementation fidelity — the degree to which PBIS is implemented as designed — is the strongest predictor of
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Assemble the PBIS leadership team; Complete the TFI; Calculate subscale scores; Identify strengths and priority areas; Develop an action plan; Implement the action plan and monitor progress; Re-administer the TFI
resourcesNeeded: TFI instrument (freely available from pbis.org); PBIS leadership team with protected meeting time; Supporting documentation (ODR data, training records, lesson plans, etc.) to inform scoring; Action plan template; Tip: Honest scoring is essential. Inflating scores produces a false sense of implementation and masks system weaknesses that directly affect students.
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Team scores items too generously*: Invite an external coach or district PBIS coordinator to participate in the scoring process to provide calibration and objectivity. - *Tier 2 and Tier 3 subscales score very low*: This is common in early implementation. Focus first on achieving 70%+ at Tier 1, a
caseStudySummary: Greenfield Elementary completed the TFI in October and scored 85% on Tier 1, 45% on Tier 2, and 30% on Tier 3. The Tier 1 score indicated strong universal supports, but the low Tier 2 score revealed that the school had no systematic process for identifying students who needed targeted intervention a
successCriteriaSummary: TFI Tier 1 subscale score reaches 70% or higher within the first year of implementation; TFI Tier 2 subscale score reaches 70% or higher within the second year; At least 80% of action plan items are completed by their target date; Data source: TFI scores (fall and spring); action plan completion tracking; correlation with ODR trends

bookTitle: Positive Behavior Support at the School Level
author: Deanne Crone & Robert Horner
functionalNeedAreas: Behavior Regulation; School Systems
strategyName: Function-Based Tier 3 Individualized Behavior Support Plan
briefOverview: Within the PBIS framework, Tier 3 is reserved for the approximately 3-5% of students who require intensive, individualized behavioral support. A Tier 3 function-based support plan involves a comprehensive functional behavioral assessment (FBA), development of a multi-component behavior intervention
evidenceBaseSummary: Crone and Horner dedicate significant attention to Tier 3 within the PBIS framework, drawing on the research of Horner, Sugai, and colleagues establishing that function-based interventions are significantly more effective than non-function-based interventions (Ingram et al., 2005; Filter & Horner, 2
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Autism spectrum disorder; Anxiety; Depression; Speech/Language impairment; Intellectual disability; OCD
implementationStepsSummary: Confirm that Tier 1 and Tier 2 supports have been implemented with fidelity; Assemble the Tier 3 team; Conduct a comprehensive FBA; Develop the multi-component BIP; Train all implementers; Implement and collect progress monitoring data; Review data and adjust the plan
resourcesNeeded: FBA interview protocols (e.g., Functional Assessment Interview form by O'Neill et al., 1997); Direct observation forms (ABC recording, scatterplot); BIP template aligned with district and IDEA requirements; Data collection and graphing tools; Team meeting time (initial plus biweekly/monthly)
challengesSolutionsSummary: - *Lack of behavioral expertise on the team*: Pursue district-level support (many districts have BCBA consultants), university partnerships, or online FBA training for school psychologists. - *Plan is too complex for teachers to implement consistently*: Simplify to 3-5 key strategies and create a on
caseStudySummary: Jasmine, a 10th-grade student with autism spectrum disorder and anxiety, engaged in elopement (leaving the classroom without permission) averaging 3 times per day, despite receiving CICO (Tier 2) for 8 weeks with minimal improvement. The Tier 3 team conducted a comprehensive FBA including teacher in
successCriteriaSummary: Target behavior decreases by at least 50% within 6-8 weeks of BIP implementation; Replacement behavior is used independently at least daily within 4 weeks; BIP is implemented with at least 80% fidelity (measured by fidelity checklist); Data source: Direct observation frequency data (graphed weekly); BIP fidelity checklist (completed biweekly); office referral and attendance data

author: Allen Mendler
functionalNeedAreas: Engagement; Motivation
briefOverview: This strategy involves explicitly connecting academic content and classroom expectations to what the student already values, cares about, or aspires to in their life outside of school. Mendler argues that students who appear "unmotivated" are actually highly motivated — just not by what school is of
evidenceBaseSummary: Mendler draws on self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985), which identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as the three fundamental psychological needs underlying intrinsic motivation. Research by Eccles and Wigfield (2002) on expectancy-value theory demonstrates that students engage in
applicableDiagnoses: ADHD; Specific learning disabilities; Depression
implementationStepsSummary: Discover the student's values and interests; Map values to current curriculum; Deliver the relevance connection explicitly; Offer value-aligned choices; Revisit and update the values map; Celebrate value-aligned effort
resourcesNeeded: Student interest inventory or conversation guide; Curriculum map with "relevance connection" column added; Tip: Relevance connections do not need to be elaborate. Even a brief verbal connection ("This skill is used in...") can significantly increase engagement.; Tip: Avoid condescension. A student who wants to be a rapper should hear "Lyricists use complex vocabulary and meter — this poetry unit is going to build those skills" rather than "Maybe you should focus more on your schoolwork."; Adaptation for students who say they do not care about anything: Focus on relationships rather than content. "I care about how you're doing" can be more motivating than any curriculum connection.

bookTitle: Essentials of Dyslexia Assessment and Intervention
author: Nancy Mather & Barbara Wendling
functionalNeedAreas: Word recognition, spelling, vocabulary, reading comprehension
briefOverview: Teaches students to identify, analyze, and manipulate meaningful units (morphemes) within words including prefixes, suffixes, and roots, providing an additional decoding route beyond phoneme-grapheme correspondences for complex multisyllabic words.
evidenceBaseSummary: Meta-analyses show effect sizes d=0.50-0.65 for morphological instruction on word reading and spelling (Carlisle 2010; Goodwin & Ahn 2013). Morphological awareness is an independent predictor of reading comprehension beyond phonological awareness.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; SLD in Written Expression; Language-Based LD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Pre-assess morphological knowledge (10-15 min); 2) Introduce morpheme concept with manipulatives (15-20 min); 3) Guided word-building practice (15 min/session); 4) Morpheme matrix/word web activities (15-20 min); 5) Apply analysis to connected text (ongoing); 6) Spelling integration with morpheme boxes (10-15 min); 7) Biweekly progress monitoring probes (10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Color-coded morpheme cards; morpheme matrix templates; high-frequency prefix/suffix/root lists; word-building journal; progress monitoring probes; grade-level texts
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Confusion of morphemes with syllables - explicitly contrast meaning chunks vs. sound chunks with sorting activities; 2) Limited vocabulary impedes root connections - begin with transparent familiar families before Latin/Greek roots; 3) Avoidance of multisyllabic words - normalize difficulty, start with two-morpheme words, use reward system
caseStudySummary: Marcus, Grade 5, Dyslexia & SLD in Reading. Multisyllabic word accuracy improved from 35% to 72% over 12 weeks (3x/week, 30 min). Vocabulary subtest increased 1.2 SD. Science comprehension rose from 55% to 74%.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% accuracy on 20-item morpheme segmentation probe (biweekly probe); 2) >=70% multisyllabic word accuracy in connected text (monthly running record); 3) >=75% accuracy defining unfamiliar words via structural analysis (quarterly vocabulary measure)

bookTitle: Essentials of Dyslexia Assessment and Intervention
author: Nancy Mather & Barbara Wendling
functionalNeedAreas: Automatic word recognition, sight word acquisition, spelling retention, reading fluency
briefOverview: Explicitly teaches students to form strong connections between pronunciation, spelling, and meaning to store words in long-term memory for instant retrieval, leveraging phoneme-level analysis rather than rote whole-word memorization.
evidenceBaseSummary: Based on Share's self-teaching hypothesis and Ehri's phase theory. Students with dyslexia require 4-8x more exposures for word storage (Ehri 2014). Explicit orthographic mapping produces effect sizes d=0.70-0.90 for word retention vs. whole-word memorization.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; SLD in Written Expression
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select 5-8 target words from instructional text (5 min); 2) Phoneme counting/identification (5-8 min); 3) Grapheme mapping on sound box template (10 min); 4) Meaning connection via definition/sentence (3-5 min); 5) Retrieval practice without model (5-8 min); 6) Contextual reading with automaticity check (5-7 min); 7) Cumulative flash review each session (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Sound box/Elkonin box templates (laminated); dry-erase markers; word cards; student word mastery log; instructional-level passages; timer/stopwatch
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Miscounting phonemes with digraphs - use mirrors and articulatory awareness, verify count before mapping; 2) Words lost between sessions - implement spaced repetition schedule with reconstruction practice; 3) Frustration with multi-step process - emphasize quality over quantity, use visual progress tracker
caseStudySummary: Aisha, Grade 3, Dyslexia. Mastered 87 new sight words in 10 weeks (4x/week, 20 min). ORF increased from 32 to 58 WCPM. Began voluntarily reading during independent reading time.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=8 new automatic sight words per week at 1-second recognition (weekly flash card log); 2) ORF increases >=15 WCPM over 8-week cycle (biweekly CBM probe); 3) >=90% accuracy mapping phonemes to graphemes on novel words (monthly generalization probe)

bookTitle: Essentials of Dyslexia Assessment and Intervention
author: Nancy Mather & Barbara Wendling
functionalNeedAreas: Spelling accuracy, written expression, phoneme-grapheme mastery, encoding
briefOverview: Requires students to hear a word, repeat it, segment into phonemes, name each letter while writing, and read the completed word, forging robust phonological-orthographic connections through simultaneous auditory, visual, articulatory, and motor engagement.
evidenceBaseSummary: Structured spelling dictation outperforms traditional study-test methods with effect sizes d=0.60-0.85 (Graham & Santangelo 2014). Berninger's research confirms integrating handwriting with phonological segmentation activates neural circuits critical for orthographic learning in dyslexia.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Written Expression; SLD in Reading; Dysgraphia (with modifications)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select 10-15 words aligned to phonics scope and sequence (5 min prep); 2) Dictate word in sentence context (1 min/word); 3) Student repeats and segments orally (30 sec/word); 4) Student writes while naming each letter aloud (30 sec/word); 5) Student reads word back for verification (15 sec/word); 6) Immediate corrective feedback on errors (1-2 min/error); 7) Score and analyze errors by type (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Structured phonics scope and sequence; dictation word lists; prepared dictation sentences; lined paper/spelling notebook; error analysis tracking sheet; pencils and erasers
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Student skips oral spelling step - establish routine firmly, use spell-check partner, add tapping step; 2) Demoralization by error count - frame as practice not test, use growth tracking, ensure instructional-level difficulty; 3) Limited time for daily dictation - shorten to 5-7 words (8 min) with 4-5x/week consistency
caseStudySummary: Jaylen, Grade 4, Dyslexia & SLD in Written Expression. TWS-5 improved from 5th to 22nd percentile over 16 weeks (4x/week, 15 min). Dictation error rate dropped from 45% to 18%. Written paragraphs became more readable with multisyllabic words.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% accuracy on weekly 10-15 word dictation at current pattern level (weekly dictation records); 2) >=50% reduction in spelling errors by category over 12 weeks (error analysis tracking sheet); 3) >=70% of target-pattern words spelled correctly in independent writing (monthly writing sample analysis)

bookTitle: Essentials of Dyslexia Assessment and Intervention
author: Nancy Mather & Barbara Wendling
functionalNeedAreas: Vocabulary development, reading comprehension, content-area learning, word retrieval
strategyName: Vocabulary Anchoring Through Semantic Feature Analysis
briefOverview: Uses a structured visual matrix to analyze target words by mapping their features, attributes, and relationships, building deep relational word knowledge rather than superficial definitions to strengthen semantic connections supporting comprehension and expression.
evidenceBaseSummary: SFA meta-analyses show effect sizes d=0.45-0.65 for vocabulary depth and reading comprehension (Jitendra et al. 2004). Aligns with Lexical Quality Hypothesis requiring high-quality word representations across phonological, orthographic, and semantic dimensions. Addresses Matthew Effect vocabulary gaps.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading Comprehension; Language-Based LD; Developmental Language Disorder (comorbid)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select 5-8 conceptually related vocabulary words from content unit (5 min); 2) Create SFA grid with words and features (5 min); 3) Activate prior knowledge via KWL chart (5-8 min); 4) Guided grid completion with +/- marking and justification (15-20 min); 5) Analyze patterns and write comparison sentences (10 min); 6) Generate feature-based definitions (10-15 min); 7) End-of-unit reassessment from memory (15 min)
resourcesNeeded: Blank SFA grid templates; content-area texts (with audio access); student vocabulary notebook; KWL chart template; educational videos (optional); dictionary/glossary
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Student cannot read content text - provide read-aloud/audio/TTS to separate decoding from vocabulary task; 2) Difficulty generating definitions from grid - provide sentence frames and model first definition; 3) Mechanical grid completion - require verbal justification, add ambiguous items for deeper discussion
caseStudySummary: Sophia, Grade 6, Dyslexia & SLD in Reading Comprehension. Vocabulary definition accuracy improved from 25% to 78% across 4 content units over 14 weeks. Content comprehension rose from 40% to 71%. PPVT-5 standard score increased from 82 to 91.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% correct feature identification on SFA grid from memory (end-of-unit grid assessment); 2) >=75% accurate feature-based definitions rated by rubric (monthly vocabulary notebook scoring); 3) >=70% of target words used correctly in written response (end-of-unit writing scored for vocabulary use)

bookTitle: Essentials of Dyslexia Assessment and Intervention
author: Nancy Mather & Barbara Wendling
functionalNeedAreas: Reading comprehension, metacognitive awareness, self-regulation, fix-up strategy use
briefOverview: Teaches students to actively monitor understanding while reading - clicks represent smooth comprehension, clunks signal confusion - and to apply specific fix-up strategies (reread, read ahead, analyze word parts, seek help) when comprehension breaks down.
evidenceBaseSummary: Self-monitoring interventions produce effect sizes d=0.60-0.80 for reading comprehension in students with LD (Berkeley et al. 2010). Click and Clunk validated in multiple RCTs as part of Collaborative Strategic Reading (Klingner & Vaughn 1999). IES practice guide recommends comprehension monitoring as evidence-based.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading Comprehension; ADHD (comorbid); Language-Based LD
