implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce Click/Clunk metaphor with think-aloud modeling (10-15 min); 2) Teach four fix-up strategies with strategy card (15 min); 3) Guided practice with sticky notes and clunk log (15-20 min/session); 4) Partner practice with peer coaching (15-20 min); 5) Independent application with clunk log and summary writing (20 min); 6) Generalize across content areas (ongoing); 7) Fade supports, assess via periodic think-alouds (5-10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Click and Clunk strategy card (laminated); sticky notes; two-column clunk log; instructional-level texts across content areas; comprehension self-rating scale; summary template
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Too many clunks overwhelm student - teach big clunk/small clunk triage for main idea vs. detail; 2) Student does not recognize breakdowns - use paragraph-level comprehension checks to build awareness before adding fix-ups; 3) Mechanical strategy use without resolution - require paraphrasing of resolved meaning, model genuine thinking process
caseStudySummary: Daniel, Grade 7, Dyslexia & ADHD. Independently identified 3-4 clunks per page and resolved 80% using fix-ups after 10 weeks (3x/week, 25 min). QRI independent level rose from grade 3 to grade 5. Began asking clarifying questions in social studies.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=3 self-identified clunks per page during think-aloud (monthly think-aloud with observation checklist); 2) >=75% of clunks resolved with fix-up strategy (biweekly clunk log analysis + teacher questioning); 3) Reading comprehension improves >=1 grade level on IRI over 10 weeks (pre/post QRI or equivalent)

author: Louisa Moats
functionalNeedAreas: Phoneme discrimination, decoding accuracy, spelling, phonics knowledge
briefOverview: Goes beyond basic phonics to teach students to map individual phonemes to graphemes with explicit attention to articulatory features, position-based spelling patterns, and the layered logic of English spelling, using speech production knowledge to clarify confusable phonemes.
evidenceBaseSummary: English orthography is approximately 84% predictable when phoneme position, stress, and morphology are considered (Hanna et al. 1966). Structured linguistic approaches show effect sizes d=0.50-0.70 vs. traditional phonics (Moats & Foorman 2003). Articulatory phonetics instruction reduces decoding errors for confusable phonemes.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; SLD in Written Expression; Speech-Language Impairment (comorbid)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess phoneme awareness and grapheme knowledge with survey (15-20 min); 2) Teach phoneme identity through articulation with mirrors (10-15 min/pair); 3) Introduce grapheme options with position/pattern rules (10-15 min/phoneme); 4) Practice with word sorts by grapheme pattern (10 min/sort); 5) Dictation with explicit grapheme decision-making (10-15 min); 6) Connected text application with highlighting and sentence writing (10-15 min); 7) Cumulative mastery assessment via dictation probes (10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Phoneme-grapheme survey; articulation reference chart with mouth diagrams; hand mirror; correspondence chart with position rules; word sort cards; mastery tracking chart; dictation word lists
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Difficulty perceiving voicing contrasts - use throat check for vibration and minimal pair discrimination practice; 2) Overwhelmed by multiple grapheme options - teach most frequent 'best bet' spelling first, add alternatives gradually; 3) Rules learned in isolation not applied in writing - include transfer tasks every session, use spelling strategy reminder card
caseStudySummary: Elena, Grade 2, Dyslexia & Speech-Language Impairment. Phoneme discrimination improved from 62% to 94% over 12 weeks (4x/week, 20 min). CVC decoding accuracy rose from 58% to 85%. Spelling of target correspondences improved from 40% to 78%.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=85% accuracy selecting correct grapheme by position/pattern rules (biweekly 15-item probe); 2) >=80% accuracy spelling dictated words with mastered correspondences (weekly 10-word dictation probe); 3) >=90% phoneme discrimination on previously confused pairs (monthly 20-pair auditory assessment)

author: Louisa Moats
functionalNeedAreas: Multisyllabic word decoding, reading fluency, vowel accuracy
strategyName: Syllable Type Instruction With Six Syllable Types Framework
briefOverview: Teaches students to recognize the six syllable types of English (closed, open, VCe, vowel team, r-controlled, consonant-le), understand how each type determines vowel sound, and use syllable division rules to decode unfamiliar multisyllabic words systematically.
evidenceBaseSummary: Multisyllabic word difficulty is a hallmark of persistent dyslexia (Archer et al. 2003). Syllable-based instruction produces effect sizes d=0.55-0.75 (Bhatt et al. 2009). English syllable types reliably predict vowel pronunciation. Validated programs (REWARDS, Wilson) incorporate syllable type instruction as core component.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; SLD in Reading Fluency
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Pre-assess syllable knowledge with 10 multisyllabic words (10-15 min); 2) Teach each syllable type explicitly with color-coding (2-3 sessions/type, 15 min each); 3) Practice syllable type identification with sorting (10 min/session); 4) Teach syllable division rules VCCV, VCV, VCCCV, VV (15 min/rule, 2-3 sessions); 5) Guided multisyllabic word analysis with checklist (15-20 min); 6) Apply to connected text during guided reading (ongoing); 7) Biweekly 20-word mastery probes (10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Syllable type reference chart (color-coded); word cards for sorting; syllable division checklist card (laminated); dry-erase board and markers; multisyllabic word probe lists; progress monitoring graph template
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Open/closed syllable confusion in VCV words - teach 'first try open' flexible strategy with real vs. nonsense word check; 2) Isolation skills don't transfer to context - use decode-and-read bridge activity from word list to sentence; 3) Robot reading without blending syllables - practice slow-medium-fast repetition and scoop reading for prosody
caseStudySummary: Tyler, Grade 4, Dyslexia & SLD in Reading Fluency. Multisyllabic word accuracy improved from 30% to 68% over 14 weeks (4x/week, 20 min). ORF increased from 52 to 78 WCPM. Reported feeling less scared of big words and stopped skipping long words.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=85% accuracy identifying syllable types within multisyllabic words (biweekly analysis probe); 2) >=75% accuracy reading untrained multisyllabic word lists (biweekly timed probe); 3) ORF increases >=20 WCPM over 12-week cycle (biweekly DIBELS/AIMSweb probe)

author: Louisa Moats
functionalNeedAreas: Sound blending, word-level decoding, phonological processing, early reading accuracy
briefOverview: Teaches students to sustain each sound and connect it without pause to the next, producing a continuous stream of phonation rather than isolated choppy sounds with schwa insertions, directly addressing the blending bottleneck in early reading for students with phonological deficits.
evidenceBaseSummary: Many students with dyslexia can segment but cannot blend phonemes (Torgesen et al. 2001). Continuous blending shows effect sizes d=0.60-0.80 vs. segmented blending (Gonzalez-Frey & Ehri 2021). Core feature of validated programs (UFLI Foundations, FCRR interventions). Eliminating schwa insertions directly reduces decoding errors.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Basic Reading Skills; Developmental Language Disorder (comorbid); Speech-Language Impairment (with phonological component)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess blending ability with 10 segmented words (5-10 min); 2) Teach stretchy vs. bumpy sound categories with sorting (10 min); 3) Model connected phonation with continuous-sound-only words (10 min/session); 4) Introduce stop sounds in connected phonation (10 min/session); 5) Practice with printed words using finger sweep (10 min/session); 6) Increase word complexity CVC to CCVCC to two-syllable (10-15 min, advance every 1-2 weeks); 7) Transfer to connected decodable text with weekly probes (ongoing, 5 min probe)
resourcesNeeded: Letter cards categorized stretchy/bumpy; finger-sweep word cards; word lists by complexity level; decodable texts; visual reference card with mouth pictures; weekly 20-word decoding probe lists
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Schwa insertion after stop consonants - demonstrate contrast explicitly, teach whispering stop sounds and immediate connection; 2) Can blend orally but not with print - use letter tiles pushed together as bridge, transition to arrows under words; 3) Restarts after blending first two sounds - practice chain blending adding one sound at a time, use rubber band metaphor
caseStudySummary: Kayla, Grade 1, Dyslexia (early identification). CVC accuracy improved from 15% to 76% over 8 weeks (daily, 15 min). DIBELS NWF increased from 8 to 34 CLS. Moved from isolated sounds to 12 whole words per minute. Began attempting to decode independently.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% CVC blending accuracy with connected phonation quality (weekly blending probe with teacher observation); 2) >=75% accuracy on decodable words at current complexity within 5 sec/word (weekly timed decoding probe); 3) NWF score increases >=20 CLS over 8 weeks (biweekly DIBELS NWF probe)

author: Louisa Moats
functionalNeedAreas: Spelling accuracy, encoding, written expression, understanding orthographic system
strategyName: Explicit Instruction in English Spelling Conventions and Rules
briefOverview: Teaches the specific conventions and rules of English spelling - floss rule, drop-e rule, doubling rule, soft c/g rule, position-based constraints - through explicit systematic instruction rather than incidental exposure, providing the cognitive framework dyslexic students need for accurate spelling.
evidenceBaseSummary: English spelling is approximately 84% predictable when position, stress, and morphology are considered (Hanna et al. 1966). Explicit spelling rule instruction shows effect sizes d=0.55-0.75 vs. implicit approaches (Graham & Santangelo 2014). Treiman's research confirms children can learn position-based constraints when explicitly taught. IDA recommends structured literacy with embedded conventions.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Written Expression; SLD in Reading; Dysgraphia (with modifications)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess current spelling rule knowledge with qualitative inventory (15-20 min); 2) Introduce one rule explicitly with linguistic rationale (10 min); 3) Guided discovery with word sorts of 20 words (10-15 min); 4) Application through dictation with rule-stating requirement (10 min); 5) Contrastive practice with rule vs. exception pairs (10 min); 6) Apply in sentence writing with self-editing (10-15 min); 7) Cumulative rule review and mixed dictation assessment (10 min review + 10 min probe)
resourcesNeeded: Qualitative spelling inventory; spelling rule reference cards; binder ring/rule flip book; word sort cards by convention; dictation word lists; student writing journal; cumulative assessment probes
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Overgeneralization of rules to exceptions - explicitly teach rule boundaries and maintain small exception list per rule; 2) Cannot remember multiple rules simultaneously - introduce max one rule/week, use mnemonics and visual anchors, provide rule ring reference; 3) Student sees rules as arbitrary - share etymological stories and linguistic rationale to make conventions meaningful
caseStudySummary: Brandon, Grade 5, Dyslexia & SLD in Written Expression. Developmental spelling stage advanced 2 years (Within Word Pattern to Syllables and Affixes) over 18 weeks (4x/week, 15 min). Convention-governed word accuracy improved from 38% to 81%. Spelling errors per page dropped from 12 to 4.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=85% accuracy on mixed-rule cumulative dictation probe (biweekly 15-word probe); 2) Spelling stage advances >=1 stage on qualitative inventory over 16 weeks (pre/post qualitative spelling inventory); 3) >=60% of convention errors self-corrected during editing (monthly writing sample draft vs. edited comparison)

author: Louisa Moats
functionalNeedAreas: Reading fluency, comprehension, oral reading expression, syntactic awareness
strategyName: Sentence-Level Reading Fluency Through Prosody Instruction
briefOverview: Explicitly teaches students to attend to punctuation, syntactic structures, and meaning cues that signal how text should sound when read aloud, addressing the persistent monotone word-by-word reading that impairs comprehension even after decoding accuracy is remediated.
evidenceBaseSummary: Prosody correlates strongly with comprehension r=0.50-0.70 even controlling for accuracy and rate (Miller & Schwanenflugel 2008). Students with dyslexia show persistent prosodic deficits after accuracy remediation (Schwanenflugel et al. 2004). Prosody interventions produce effect sizes d=0.40-0.60 for comprehension (Rasinski et al. 2009). NRP identified fluency as essential component.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading Fluency; SLD in Reading Comprehension; Autism Spectrum Disorder (comorbid)
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess baseline prosody with NAEP or Multidimensional Fluency Scale (5-10 min); 2) Teach punctuation as prosodic signals with practice sentences (10 min); 3) Introduce phrase-cued/chunked text with modeling (10-15 min/passage); 4) Echo reading model-and-imitate at sentence then paragraph level (10 min/session); 5) Reader's Theater with repeated practice and performance (15-20 min, 3-4 sessions/script); 6) Record and self-evaluate with simplified rubric (10-15 min); 7) Generalize to independent reading with periodic check-ins (3-5 min + monthly 10 min assessment)
resourcesNeeded: NAEP or Multidimensional Fluency Scale rubric; phrase-cued text passages; Reader's Theater scripts; audio recording device; simplified self-evaluation prosody rubric; instructional-level passages; punctuation reference chart
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Decoding too effortful for prosody - use independent-level text (>=95% accuracy) or previously-practiced passages; 2) Exaggerated artificial expression - model natural conversational prosody, compare to real conversation; 3) Self-consciousness about reading aloud - begin one-on-one, use self-recording before peer practice, allow role choice in Reader's Theater
caseStudySummary: Maria, Grade 3, Dyslexia. MFS prosody score increased from 1.5 to 3.2 over 12 weeks (3x/week, 20 min). ORF increased from 65 to 84 WCPM. GORT-5 comprehension rose from 25th to 42nd percentile. Began reading aloud to younger sibling at home.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=3/4 on MFS expression and phrasing dimensions (monthly formal prosody assessment); 2) ORF increases >=15 WCPM over 10-week cycle (biweekly DIBELS/AIMSweb probe); 3) Reading comprehension increases >=10 percentile points on standardized measure over 12 weeks (pre/post GORT-5 or WIAT-4)

bookTitle: Multisensory Teaching of Basic Language Skills
author: Judith Birsh
functionalNeedAreas: Phonological awareness, letter-sound correspondence, decoding, spelling
briefOverview: Uses simultaneous visual, auditory, and kinesthetic-tactile pathways to teach phoneme-grapheme correspondences. Students see, say, and trace/write letters simultaneously. Instruction is systematic, sequential, and cumulative.
evidenceBaseSummary: Validated by National Reading Panel (2000), Ritchey & Goeke (2006), IDA; neuroimaging research by Shaywitz (2003) confirms multisensory instruction strengthens left-hemisphere reading circuits. Recognized as Tier 2/3 evidence-based intervention.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; Language-Based LD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Phonogram review with cards (5 min), 2) Introduce new phonogram (3 min), 3) Skywriting/tracing while saying sounds (5 min), 4) Blending drill with letter board (7 min), 5) Dictation/encoding practice (8 min), 6) Connected decodable text reading (7 min), 7) Mastery check with randomized cards (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Phonogram drill cards, sand tray, lined paper, decodable readers, progress monitoring sheet, letter tiles
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Frustration with slow pace - use visual progress ladder; 2) Fine motor difficulty with tracing - substitute skywriting or textured cards; 3) Memorizing instead of decoding - use cover-reveal technique
caseStudySummary: Marcus, Grade 2, Dyslexia: DIBELS NWF rose from 8th to 35th percentile; identified all 44 taught phonograms at 95% accuracy; ORF increased from 15 to 42 WCPM over 16 weeks of 4x/week sessions.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=90% accuracy on phonogram drill (daily tracking sheet); 2) >=80% CVC/CCVC word decoding on weekly probes; 3) >=1.5 WCPM/week growth slope on DIBELS/AIMSweb ORF probes

bookTitle: Multisensory Teaching of Basic Language Skills
author: Judith Birsh
functionalNeedAreas: Multisyllabic word reading, vocabulary, reading comprehension, spelling
strategyName: Morphological Awareness Training for Vocabulary and Decoding
briefOverview: Explicitly teaches recognition and manipulation of morphemes (prefixes, suffixes, roots) for decoding multisyllabic words and inferring meaning. Moves from Anglo-Saxon to Latin/Greek roots in structured sequence. Students physically break apart and reassemble words.
evidenceBaseSummary: Supported by Bowers, Kirby & Deacon (2010), Goodwin & Ahn (2013) meta-analysis showing significant effects on comprehension, vocabulary, spelling, and decoding for students with LD. Aligns with IDA Structured Literacy recommendations.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; Language-Based LD; Developmental Language Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce target morpheme with card (4 min), 2) Word building with morpheme tiles (6 min), 3) Co-create word matrix (5 min), 4) Word sort with distractors (5 min), 5) Sentence-level application (6 min), 6) Text reading with morpheme spotting (7 min), 7) Cumulative review quiz (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Morpheme flashcards, color-coded morpheme tiles, word matrix templates, word sort cards, reading passages, student morpheme notebook
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Confusing prefixes with look-alikes - teach peel-off test; 2) No transfer to independent reading - provide morpheme detective bookmark; 3) Difficulty memorizing Latin/Greek roots - use mnemonic images and illustrated dictionary
caseStudySummary: Aisha, Grade 5, SLD in Reading: Multisyllabic word accuracy rose from 42% to 88%; PPVT-5 increased from 18th to 34th percentile; MAZE comprehension improved from 15th to 30th percentile over 12 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=85% accuracy on morpheme segmentation quiz (weekly); 2) >=80% correct definitions on vocabulary probe (bi-weekly); 3) >=10 percentile point comprehension growth over 12 weeks (AIMSweb MAZE)

bookTitle: Multisensory Teaching of Basic Language Skills
author: Judith Birsh
functionalNeedAreas: Spelling accuracy, phoneme-grapheme mapping, orthographic memory, spelling rule application
strategyName: Multisensory Structured Spelling Instruction (Simultaneous Oral Spelling - SOS)
briefOverview: Students hear a word, repeat it, segment into phonemes, name letters while writing each one, and read the completed word. Integrates auditory, visual, and kinesthetic channels simultaneously during spelling within a rule-based curriculum.
evidenceBaseSummary: Core component of OG methodology; supported by Cox (1992), Wilson Reading System validation, NRP (2000). Berninger et al. (2008) showed combining handwriting with phonological segmentation produces stronger spelling outcomes than single-modality instruction.
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; Dysgraphia; SLD in Written Expression; Language-Based LD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Review spelling rule with anchor chart (4 min), 2) Teacher models SOS procedure (2 min), 3) Guided SOS practice with 5 words (8 min), 4) Error analysis and correction (4 min), 5) Dictated sentences (8 min), 6) Proofreading practice (4 min), 7) Mastery assessment of 10 words (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Spelling rule anchor charts, lined paper with raised lines, dictation word lists, student progress chart, spelling pattern reference card
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Rushing through SOS - use whisper phone and checklist card; 2) No transfer to free writing - create personal spelling wall; 3) Overwhelmed by rules - limit to one new rule per week with cumulative Rules I Know ring
caseStudySummary: Jordan, Grade 3, Dyslexia/Dysgraphia: Spelling accuracy on taught patterns rose from 35% to 87%; WIAT-4 Spelling improved from 5th to 22nd percentile; free-writing errors reduced from 12 to 5 per 50-word sample over 14 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=85% spelling accuracy on weekly dictation (10-word test); 2) >=50% reduction in free-writing spelling errors over 12 weeks (bi-weekly samples); 3) >=80% independent SOS self-check use (weekly teacher observation)

bookTitle: Multisensory Teaching of Basic Language Skills
author: Judith Birsh
functionalNeedAreas: Rhyming, sound blending, sound segmentation, phonemic awareness, connecting sounds to print
briefOverview: Provides explicit instruction across the full PA continuum from syllable to phoneme-level operations. Each level is taught to mastery using manipulatives, body movements, and visual markers before advancing. Critical foundational skill for phonics effectiveness.
evidenceBaseSummary: NRP (2000) identified PA instruction with effect size of 0.86 for students with reading disabilities. Torgesen et al. (1999) prevented reading failure in 95% of at-risk students. PA is the strongest single predictor of reading success (Stanovich, 1986; Wagner & Torgesen, 1987).
applicableDiagnoses: Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; Developmental Language Disorder; Speech-Language Impairment
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess current PA level with PAST screener (10 min initial), 2) Syllable-level clapping/tapping (5 min), 3) Onset-rime identification and sorting (5 min), 4) Phoneme blending with tokens (5 min), 5) Phoneme segmentation with Elkonin boxes (6 min), 6) Phoneme deletion/substitution with tokens (6 min), 7) Bridge to print with letter tiles (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: PAST screener, tokens/counters, Elkonin boxes, picture cards, letter tiles, PA continuum progress monitoring sheet
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Cannot segment at phoneme level - insert onset-rime step and use continuant sounds; 2) Confuses PA with letter tasks - keep activities purely oral until bridging step; 3) Co-occurring speech impairment - consult SLP and allow pointing instead of production
caseStudySummary: Sofia, Grade 1, Developmental Language Disorder/at-risk Dyslexia: PAST advanced to automatic level for blending/segmenting; DIBELS First Sound Fluency rose from 8 to 32; began successfully decoding CVC words in print after 10 weeks of daily 15-min sessions.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=90% phoneme blending accuracy within 3 sec (weekly 10-item probe); 2) >=90% phoneme segmentation accuracy (weekly 10-item probe with Elkonin boxes); 3) >=2 level advancement on PA continuum in 10 weeks (PAST pre/post)

bookTitle: Multisensory Teaching of Basic Language Skills
author: Judith Birsh
functionalNeedAreas: Expository text comprehension, main idea identification, summarization, information organization
strategyName: Text Structure and Comprehension Mapping for Expository Text
briefOverview: Teaches students to identify expository text structures (cause-effect, compare-contrast, sequence, problem-solution, description) using signal words and graphic organizers. Multisensory components include physical manipulation of text segments, color-coding, and constructing organizers with movable pieces.
evidenceBaseSummary: Meyer & Ray (2011) showed text structure instruction significantly improves comprehension. Williams et al. (2005, 2007) demonstrated effectiveness for students with LD with transfer to novel passages. NRP (2000) identified graphic organizers and comprehension monitoring as effective strategies.
applicableDiagnoses: SLD in Reading Comprehension; Dyslexia; Language-Based LD; ADHD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce one text structure with anchor chart and signal words (6 min), 2) Signal word hunt in passage (4 min), 3) Model graphic organizer completion with think-aloud (5 min), 4) Guided practice with sticky note manipulatives (8 min), 5) Oral summary from organizer (4 min), 6) Written summary from organizer (6 min), 7) Independent mixed-structure identification (7 min)
resourcesNeeded: Text structure anchor charts, color-coded signal word cards, graphic organizer templates, sticky notes/sentence strips, leveled expository passages, sentence frame cards
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Mixed-structure passages - teach dominant vs. secondary structure; 2) Cannot use organizer independently - implement gradual release with procedural checklist; 3) No transfer to content areas - collaborate with general ed teacher and pre-teach structures
caseStudySummary: David, Grade 4, SLD in Reading/ADHD: Text structure identification rose from 20% to 85% accuracy; written summaries included 4/5 key details (up from 1/5); MAZE improved from 14th to 32nd percentile; science assessment scores rose from 55% to 74% over 10 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% text structure identification on novel passages (bi-weekly 5-passage probe); 2) >=4/5 key details in written summaries (bi-weekly rubric); 3) >=15 percentile point comprehension growth over 10 weeks (AIMSweb MAZE)

bookTitle: Learning Disabilities and Related Mild Disabilities
author: Janet Lerner
functionalNeedAreas: Severe word recognition deficits, sight word vocabulary, reading motivation, written expression
strategyName: Fernald VAKT Whole-Word Method for Severe Reading Disability
briefOverview: Language-experience approach where students select personally meaningful words, trace them in large format while saying syllables, write from memory, and use them in self-generated stories. Engages all four sensory modalities. Effective for students who have failed phonics-first instruction.
evidenceBaseSummary: Developed by Fernald (1943); supported by Berninger et al. (2006) on writing-reading integration. Recognized by IDA as alternative multisensory approach. Thorpe & Borden (1985) demonstrated effectiveness for building sight vocabulary and motivation in students with severe word recognition deficits.
applicableDiagnoses: Severe Dyslexia; SLD in Reading; Mild Intellectual Disability; Language-Based LD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Student selects a word to learn (2 min), 2) Teacher models tracing while saying syllables (1 min), 3) Student traces and vocalizes until confident (5 min), 4) Write word from memory (3 min), 5) File word alphabetically in personal word box (1 min), 6) Write story using new and mastered words (8 min), 7) Re-read typed story next day and review (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: Large paper strips, thick crayons/markers, lined writing paper, alphabetized word file box, blank index cards, computer/printer for typing stories
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Older student resists tracing as babyish - use tablet/iPad or cursive, frame as brain-based technique; 2) Student wants very long complex words - honor choice and break into syllable chunks; 3) Repeated memory errors - increase tracings and add mental visualization step
caseStudySummary: Tomas, Grade 4, Severe Dyslexia (treatment-resistant): Word file grew to 127 mastered words; wrote 22 personal stories; San Diego Quick Assessment advanced from pre-primer to late 2nd grade; reading attitude improved from 24/80 to 58/80 over 18 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=8-10 new retained sight words per week (weekly word file count with retention check); 2) >=2 stories written and fluently re-read per week (teacher log); 3) >=1.0 grade level equivalent growth in 18 weeks (San Diego Quick Assessment)

bookTitle: Learning Disabilities and Related Mild Disabilities
author: Janet Lerner
functionalNeedAreas: Comprehension monitoring, self-correction, metacognitive awareness, active reading
strategyName: Explicit Self-Monitoring Comprehension Strategy (Click and Clunk)
briefOverview: Teaches students to monitor comprehension by distinguishing clicks (understood) from clunks (confusion). When a clunk is identified, students apply fix-up strategies in a structured order. Addresses the metacognitive deficit in students with LD who read passively without recognizing comprehension breakdowns.
evidenceBaseSummary: Based on Collaborative Strategic Reading (Klingner & Vaughn, 1999; Klingner, Vaughn & Schumm, 1998) with moderate to large effect sizes. Rooted in Palincsar & Brown (1984) reciprocal teaching research. Aligns with NRP (2000) comprehension monitoring recommendations.
applicableDiagnoses: SLD in Reading Comprehension; ADHD; Language-Based LD; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce click/clunk concept with think-aloud model (5 min), 2) Teach 4 fix-up strategies with Clunk Card (6 min), 3) Guided paragraph-level practice with monitoring sheet (8 min), 4) Independent passage-level practice (8 min), 5) Post-reading review of monitoring log (5 min), 6) Peer partnership practice (6 min), 7) Generalization to content-area text with self-reflection (7 min)
resourcesNeeded: Laminated Clunk Card, click/clunk monitoring logs, green/red markers, grade-level passages, self-reflection journal, timer
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Marks everything as click - use comprehension checks and teach self-questioning; 2) Does not use fix-up strategies systematically - require physical pointing on Clunk Card; 3) Over-focuses on clunks and loses meaning - transition to paragraph-level monitoring and add whole-passage summary
caseStudySummary: Priya, Grade 6, SLD in Reading Comprehension/ADHD-I: Identified 85% of comprehension breakdowns (up from baseline); resolved 70% of clunks independently (up from 10%); MAZE improved from 12th to 28th percentile; began independently re-reading confusing sections over 12 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% accurate clunk identification (teacher-created passages with planted obstacles); 2) >=70% clunks resolved independently (bi-weekly monitoring log analysis); 3) >=15 percentile point comprehension growth over 12 weeks (AIMSweb MAZE)

bookTitle: Learning Disabilities and Related Mild Disabilities
author: Janet Lerner
functionalNeedAreas: Vocabulary memorization, content-area test performance, verbal memory compensation, academic vocabulary
strategyName: Keyword Mnemonic Strategy for Content-Area Vocabulary
briefOverview: Systematic memory technique: students recode unfamiliar words into similar-sounding keywords, create vivid mental images linking keywords to definitions, and retrieve definitions through the keyword-image chain. Powerful compensatory strategy for students who struggle with rote memorization.
evidenceBaseSummary: One of the most researched mnemonic strategies in special education; Mastropieri & Scruggs (1998), Scruggs & Mastropieri (2000) report effect sizes consistently exceeding 1.0 for vocabulary acquisition by students with LD. Can bring LD students to performance levels comparable to typically achieving peers.
applicableDiagnoses: SLD in Reading; ADHD; Language-Based LD; Mild Intellectual Disability; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce 3-step keyword method with example (5 min), 2) Teacher models with 3 vocabulary words (6 min), 3) Guided practice - student creates keywords for 3 words (8 min), 4) Elaborative rehearsal for all 6 words (5 min), 5) Independent application for 4 new words (8 min), 6) Spaced retrieval practice schedule (5 min/session), 7) Test preparation and assessment comparison (5 min review)
resourcesNeeded: Blank vocabulary cards, colored pencils/markers, content-area vocabulary lists, spaced retrieval schedule chart, vocabulary card file, sample keyword-image pairs
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Cannot generate keywords - provide 2-3 options to choose from initially; 2) Weak image-definition link - teach interactive and exaggerated imagery; 3) Remembers image but not definition - add verbal rehearsal component and include definition on card back
caseStudySummary: Jaylen, Grade 7, SLD in Reading/ADHD: Created keyword cards for 64 words; science vocabulary quiz average rose from 45% to 82%; unit test vocabulary questions improved from 40% to 78%; began independently using strategy in social studies over 8 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=80% correct on weekly vocabulary quizzes (content-area quiz scores); 2) >=75% retention after 2-week delay (bi-weekly cumulative review probe); 3) >=90% accuracy on independently created keyword cards (weekly teacher review)

bookTitle: Learning Disabilities and Related Mild Disabilities
author: Janet Lerner
functionalNeedAreas: Written expression, planning, organization, self-regulation, composition quality and length
strategyName: Written Language Process Approach Using SRSD (Self-Regulated Strategy Development for Writing)
briefOverview: Combines explicit writing strategy instruction with self-regulation techniques using mnemonic-based planning strategies (POW + TREE for persuasive writing). Students internalize strategies through a 6-stage model: develop background knowledge, discuss it, model it, memorize it, support it, independent performance.
evidenceBaseSummary: Over 100 experimental studies; Graham & Harris (2003), Graham, Harris & McKeown (2013) meta-analyses report effect sizes >1.0 for writing quality, length, and elements. What Works Clearinghouse rates SRSD as having strong evidence for writing improvement.
applicableDiagnoses: SLD in Written Expression; Dysgraphia; ADHD; Language-Based LD; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Develop background knowledge and introduce POW+TREE mnemonic (10 min), 2) Discuss strategy value and set personal goals (8 min), 3) Teacher models essay writing with think-aloud (12 min), 4) Memorize mnemonic to 100% recall (5 min), 5) Collaborative writing with gradual release (15 min/essay), 6) Independent writing with self-monitoring checklist (15 min), 7) Maintenance, fading, and generalization to new genres (ongoing)
resourcesNeeded: POW+TREE mnemonic poster and cards, graphic organizer templates, self-monitoring checklist, self-statement cue cards, writing rubric, baseline writing samples, timer
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Memorizes mnemonic but skips planning - require organizer submission before drafting; 2) All parts present but low quality - add say more step and DARE sub-strategy; 3) Dependent on graphic organizer - follow planned fading sequence from full organizer to mental planning
caseStudySummary: Elena, Grade 5, SLD in Written Expression/ADHD: Essay parts increased from 2.3 to 6.2 out of 7; word count grew from 38 to 112; quality rating improved from 2/8 to 6/8; WIAT-4 Written Expression rose from 9th to 30th percentile; spent 5 minutes planning vs. none at baseline over 8 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=6/7 essay parts in independent writing (bi-weekly POW+TREE rubric); 2) >=100% increase in essay length from baseline (bi-weekly word count); 3) >=80% of writing sessions show observable planning (weekly teacher observation log)

bookTitle: Learning Disabilities and Related Mild Disabilities
author: Janet Lerner
functionalNeedAreas: Reading fluency/rate, reading automaticity, prosody, reading stamina, fluency-comprehension link
strategyName: Repeated Reading Fluency Intervention with Self-Charting
briefOverview: Combines repeated readings of the same passage with student self-monitoring through visual WCPM charting. Addresses insufficient automaticity that consumes cognitive resources needed for comprehension. Self-charting builds motivation, metacognitive awareness, and self-regulation.
evidenceBaseSummary: Samuels (1979) original research; NRP (2000) validation; Therrien (2004) meta-analysis found mean effect size of 0.83 for transfer to unpracticed passages. Self-monitoring component supported by Shimabukuro et al. (1999) showing enhanced motivation and achievement for students with LD.
applicableDiagnoses: SLD in Reading Fluency; Dyslexia; ADHD; Language-Based LD
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Select passage and set baseline WCPM with charting (3 min), 2) Error correction and vocabulary pre-teaching (4 min), 3) Teacher modeled fluent reading (2 min), 4) Repeated reading round 2 with feedback (3 min), 5) Timed repeated reading round 3 with charting (3 min), 6) Prosody check and comprehension questions (3 min), 7) Goal-setting for next session (2 min)
resourcesNeeded: Leveled reading passages, stopwatch, student fluency graph, colored pencils, fluency scoring sheets, prosody rubric, comprehension question cards
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Sacrifices accuracy for speed - set dual criterion (95% accuracy + WCPM); 2) Bored with repeated reading - offer passage choice and partner reading; 3) No transfer to unpracticed text - use shared vocabulary passages and bi-weekly transfer probes
caseStudySummary: Kenji, Grade 3, Dyslexia/SLD in Reading Fluency: Transfer fluency on novel passages improved from 42 to 71 WCPM; practiced passage fluency averaged 98 WCPM; prosody improved from 1.5 to 3.0/4; MAZE comprehension gained 12 percentile points over 12 weeks of 4x/week 15-min sessions.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) >=30% WCPM increase on practiced passages within 4 readings (fluency graph comparison); 2) >=1.5 WCPM/week growth on novel transfer probes (bi-weekly probes); 3) >=3/4 prosody rating on repeated reading passages (teacher-rated rubric)

author: Lynn Meltzer
functionalNeedAreas: Task Initiation, Planning and Prioritization, Goal-Directed Persistence, Time Management
briefOverview: Cyclical metacognitive strategy teaching students to set explicit goals, create plans, execute with self-monitoring, and review outcomes against goals. Makes invisible cognitive processes visible and teachable across content areas.
evidenceBaseSummary: ResearchILD studies showing 20-30% improvement in academic outcomes with explicit metacognitive instruction; grounded in Flavell's metacognitive theory and Borkowski's model of metacognition and EF.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce GPDR cycle with anchor chart (15 min); 2) Model GOAL phase with I-will stems (10 min); 3) Model PLAN phase with graphic organizer (10 min); 4) Guided DO phase practice with self-monitoring checklist (15-20 min); 5) Model REVIEW phase with rubric self-scoring (10 min); 6) Peer-supported practice (20 min); 7) Whole-class debrief and journal entry (5-10 min)
resourcesNeeded: GPDR anchor chart; graphic organizer; self-monitoring checklist; metacognitive journal; visual timer; sample rubrics
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Vague goals: Provide sentence stems and goal quality checklist; 2) Resistance to review phase: Embed into grading and gamify with strategy stars; 3) Limited transfer: Use consistent GPDR language across subjects with multiple teachers
caseStudySummary: Marcus, Grade 6, ADHD and EFD. Assignment completion rose from 35% to 82% after 8 weeks. Writing rubric scores improved from 1.8/4 to 3.1/4. 60% reduction in off-task redirections.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) 80% of tasks with completed GPDR logs within 6 weeks (GPDR tracking sheet); 2) Assignment completion increases 25+ percentage points (gradebook data); 3) Planning confidence self-rating at 4/5+ (biweekly survey)

author: Lynn Meltzer
functionalNeedAreas: Cognitive Flexibility, Problem-Solving, Self-Monitoring, Set-Shifting
strategyName: BrainCogs — Cognitive Flexibility and Shifting Strategy
briefOverview: Multimedia-supported program teaching students to understand and deploy cognitive processes, particularly flexibility and set-shifting. Uses Stuck Signal recognition and strategy shift menus to help students move past cognitive rigidity.
evidenceBaseSummary: ResearchILD evaluations showing significant improvements in strategy use, cognitive flexibility, and academic self-concept; grounded in Diamond's (2013) framework of core executive functions and neuropsychological research on prefrontal cortex.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Learning Disabilities
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce cognitive cogs visual and discuss EF processes (15 min); 2) Teach Stuck Signal self-monitoring prompt (10 min); 3) Model Shift Strategy with alternative approach menu (10 min); 4) Guided practice with multi-method tasks (15 min); 5) Paired Flex Challenge with shift tracking (15-20 min); 6) Whole-class reflection on shifts (10 min); 7) Set personal flex goal and daily journal logging (5 min)
resourcesNeeded: BrainCogs visual poster; Stuck Signal cue cards; strategy shift menu; Flex Challenge activity sets; shift tracking sheet; metacognitive journal
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Difficulty recognizing being stuck: Graduated cuing from teacher to peer to self-monitoring; 2) ASD students overwhelmed by open menus: Reduce to 2-3 options with visual supports; 3) Curriculum integration: Embed BrainCogs language into existing lessons with 2-min Flex Moments
caseStudySummary: Aisha, Grade 4, ASD and EFD. Shutdown episodes decreased from 4/week to 0.5/week after 10 weeks. Independent Shift Strategy use rose from 5% to 70%. Math scores improved by 15 percentile points.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Independent strategy shifting in 75% of opportunities within 8 weeks (teacher observation checklist); 2) Frustration episodes decrease 50%+ (behavior tracking log); 3) Accurate cognitive process identification on 4/5 tasks (monthly quiz)

author: Lynn Meltzer
functionalNeedAreas: Organization, Planning, Working Memory, Self-Monitoring, Written Expression
strategyName: Personalized Strategy Instruction for Written Expression (POWER)
briefOverview: Multi-step personalized writing strategy addressing EF demands of organizing ideas, planning text structure, self-monitoring during drafting, and revising. Uses structured templates, graphic organizers, and explicit rubric-based self-checking.
evidenceBaseSummary: Draws on Graham and Harris SRSD model validated in 100+ studies; ResearchILD studies showing 1.5 rubric-point improvement vs. 0.3 for standard instruction; links EF deficits to writing difficulties.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Administer writing sample and EF self-assessment to identify bottleneck (20 min); 2) Introduce POWER framework with anchor chart (10 min); 3) Model Plan and Organize phases (15 min); 4) Student practice with individualized teacher conferencing (20 min); 5) Model Write phase with think-aloud and paragraph checklist (15 min); 6) Model Edit/Revise with self-editing checklist (15 min); 7) Independent full POWER cycle on curriculum task (30-40 min)
resourcesNeeded: POWER anchor chart; graphic organizer templates; self-monitoring paragraph checklist; self-editing checklist; writing rubric; EF self-assessment; writing folders
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Skipping planning: Require graded plan submission before drafting; 2) Working memory loss during drafting: Side-by-side organizer with color-coding; 3) Revision perceived as punitive: Reframe as professional skill with revision points
caseStudySummary: Jayden, Grade 8, ADHD and SLD-Written Expression. Writing rubric scores improved from 2.0/6 to 4.2/6 after 12 weeks. Time-on-task increased from 40% to 78%. Process artifact completion rose from 10% to 95%.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Rubric score 4/6+ for 3 consecutive assignments (teacher-scored rubric); 2) Complete all five POWER phases for 80% of assignments (portfolio review); 3) Writing confidence improves 1+ point on 5-point scale (pre/post survey)

author: Lynn Meltzer
functionalNeedAreas: Self-Concept, Motivation, Goal-Directed Persistence, Metacognitive Awareness
briefOverview: Teaches students to attribute academic outcomes to controllable factors (strategy use, effort) rather than fixed ability. Students create Effort Maps visually connecting strategies employed to outcomes, counteracting maladaptive attributions common in EF deficit students.
evidenceBaseSummary: Grounded in Weiner's attribution theory and Dweck's mindset research; ResearchILD studies showing greater strategy maintenance at 3-month follow-up with attributional retraining; meta-analyses showing d=0.61 effect on academic motivation.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Administer attribution questionnaire for baseline (10 min); 2) Teach controllable vs. uncontrollable factors with sorting activity (15 min); 3) Introduce Effort Map template (10 min); 4) Model completing Effort Map for sample scenario (10 min); 5) Students complete Effort Map for recent assignment with conferencing (15 min); 6) Strategy Spotlight class discussion (10 min); 7) Set weekly effort-strategy goal and daily tracking (5 min setup, ongoing)
resourcesNeeded: Attribution questionnaire; sorting cards; Effort Map template; Strategy Spotlight discussion protocol; weekly goal tracker; portfolio folder
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Deeply negative self-beliefs: Start with non-academic examples where effort-outcome links are clear; 2) Attributing success externally: Use video recordings of own strategy use as evidence; 3) Fixed mindset reinforcement at home: Provide parent guide and brief parent information session
caseStudySummary: Sofia, Grade 5, EFD and SLD-Reading. Academic self-concept improved from 12th to 38th percentile (MSCS). Reading attempt rate increased from 45% to 90%. ORF scores improved by 18 words per minute over 10 weeks.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) 70%+ of Effort Map entries attribute outcomes to controllable factors by week 8 (Effort Map content analysis); 2) Academic self-concept improves 15+ percentile points (pre/post MSCS); 3) Task attempt rate increases 30+ percentage points (gradebook tracking)

author: Lynn Meltzer
functionalNeedAreas: Prioritization, Time Management, Task Initiation, Organization, Emotional Regulation
strategyName: Stoplight Prioritization and Time Management System
briefOverview: Visual prioritization system using red/yellow/green classifications for urgency and importance, paired with time estimation and scheduling. Addresses the EF deficit of deciding which task to do first, reducing overwhelm and avoidance.
evidenceBaseSummary: References Barkley's model of ADHD as self-regulation disorder; ResearchILD studies showing 34% improvement in homework completion and 40% reduction in homework conflict; supported by research on visual-spatial supports for EF deficit students.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce Stoplight metaphor with visual poster and definitions (10 min); 2) Model categorizing sample assignment list with think-aloud (10 min); 3) Teach Time Guess and Check technique (10 min); 4) Students categorize own assignments with guidance (15 min); 5) Create daily action plan with time blocking (10 min); 6) Execute plan during work period with timer (20-30 min); 7) End-of-day review and accuracy tracking (5-10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Stoplight visual poster; categorization template; daily action plan template; visual timer; Time Guess and Check log; weekly tracker; planner or assignment collection tool
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Everything categorized as red: Limit red items to 2/day and teach simplified Eisenhower Matrix; 2) Time estimation errors with ADHD: Use time anchors referencing known task durations; 3) System feels babyish to older students: Reframe as professional productivity tool with adult examples
caseStudySummary: DeShawn, Grade 7, ADHD-Combined and EFD. Missing assignments decreased from 5.2 to 1.1 per grading period after 6 weeks. Homework completion rose from 48% to 85%. Time estimation accuracy improved from 30% to 72%.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Task prioritization matches teacher rating for 80%+ of assignments within 6 weeks (weekly template review); 2) Homework completion increases 30+ percentage points (gradebook data); 3) Time estimation within +/-5 min for 70% of tasks within 8 weeks (Time Guess log)

bookTitle: Self-Regulated Learning Interventions with At-Risk Youth
author: Timothy J. Cleary
functionalNeedAreas: Goal Setting, Strategic Planning, Self-Efficacy, Task Initiation
briefOverview: Microanalytic approach training students in the Forethought Phase of SRL: setting process-specific goals, developing strategic plans, and activating self-efficacy beliefs before academic tasks. Highly individualized using microanalytic assessment of each student's specific SRL deficits.
evidenceBaseSummary: Validated in multiple studies with at-risk middle and high school students showing significant improvements in self-efficacy, strategic planning, and academic performance; grounded in Zimmerman's cyclical SRL model; SRL Microanalysis demonstrates strong reliability and validity.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Conduct microanalytic assessment with forethought questions before a task (15-20 min); 2) Analyze data to identify specific forethought deficits (10 min prep); 3) Teach outcome vs. process goal distinction with conversion practice (15 min); 4) Model strategic planning with Strategy Selection Menu (10 min); 5) Introduce self-efficacy calibration with predict-perform-compare cycle (15 min); 6) Guided practice of full forethought protocol (20 min); 7) Independent forethought practice on 3 tasks over one week (5 min setup, distributed)
resourcesNeeded: Microanalytic assessment protocol; Forethought Phase recording form; process goal conversion worksheet; Strategy Selection Menu; self-efficacy calibration chart; session tracking log; domain-specific practice tasks
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Socially desirable responses during assessment: Build rapport, emphasize no wrong answers, use follow-up probes; 2) Abstract process goals: Use the 'so specific I could video it' test; 3) Miscalibrated self-efficacy: Track calibration accuracy over time as a coaching graph
caseStudySummary: Alejandro, Grade 9, ADHD and GAD, at-risk. Process goal-setting rose from 0% to 85% of math tasks after 8 weeks. Self-efficacy calibration accuracy improved from 22% to 68%. Math quiz scores increased from 52% to 74% mean.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Process-oriented goals for 80%+ of tasks within 8 weeks (Forethought recording forms); 2) Self-efficacy calibration accuracy reaches 60%+ (calibration chart vs. actual performance); 3) Domain performance improves 15+ percentage points (quiz/test scores)

bookTitle: Self-Regulated Learning Interventions with At-Risk Youth
author: Timothy J. Cleary
functionalNeedAreas: Sustained Attention, Self-Monitoring, Self-Awareness, On-Task Behavior, Strategy Execution
briefOverview: Teaches students to systematically monitor and record their own behavior and strategy use in real time during academic tasks. Uses structured self-recording tools with cueing systems, progressing from frequent external cues to internalized self-monitoring.
evidenceBaseSummary: Meta-analyses showing d=0.60-0.80 across populations; research showing combined self-monitoring and strategic instruction produces greater gains than either alone; studies with at-risk youth showing 25-40% increase in on-task behavior and 15-20% improvement in accuracy.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Establish baseline via momentary time sampling across 3 sessions (3x15-20 min); 2) Teach on-task vs. off-task distinction with examples and role-plays (15 min); 3) Introduce self-recording tool and demonstrate use (10 min); 4) Set cueing system at 3-min intervals (5 min); 5) Student practices with teacher inter-rater comparison and debrief (20 min); 6) Fade cuing intervals (3-5-10 min to self-initiated) over 4-6 weeks (ongoing); 7) Student graphs data weekly and sets performance goals (10 min/week)
resourcesNeeded: Momentary time sampling form; self-recording checklist; cueing device (vibrating timer/app); on-task/off-task definition card; graphing template; weekly goal-setting form
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Self-recording becomes a distraction: Simplify to single check mark, reduce response effort; 2) Inflated self-recording: Periodic inter-rater checks with non-punitive discrepancy discussions; 3) Dependence on external cue: Systematic fading schedule with internal check-in prompt training
caseStudySummary: Brianna, Grade 10, ADHD-Inattentive and EFD, at-risk. On-task behavior increased from 38% to 79% after 6 weeks. Inter-rater agreement reached 85%. Assignment completion rose from 40% to 82%. Biology quiz scores improved by 12 percentage points.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) On-task behavior reaches 75%+ within 6 weeks (self-recording checklist verified by teacher); 2) Inter-rater agreement reaches 80%+ (student vs. teacher interval data); 3) Assignment completion increases 30+ percentage points (teacher tracking form)

bookTitle: Self-Regulated Learning Interventions with At-Risk Youth
author: Timothy J. Cleary
functionalNeedAreas: Self-Evaluation, Metacognitive Reflection, Emotional Regulation, Adaptive Attribution, Growth Orientation
strategyName: Self-Reflection Protocols and Causal Attribution Training
briefOverview: Structured protocol for the Self-Reflection Phase of SRL, guiding students through productive self-evaluation and causal attribution after task completion. Uses three reflection questions and Attribution Pie visual to build adaptive inference-making and reduce maladaptive self-reflection patterns.
evidenceBaseSummary: Zimmerman and Moylan research showing self-reflection is the most neglected SRL phase; structured protocols improve subsequent forethought quality by 30-45%; Borkowski, Weyhing, and Carr attribution retraining research demonstrates greater strategy persistence.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce productive vs. unproductive self-reflection with examples (10 min); 2) Teach three-question Reflection Protocol (10 min); 3) Model completing protocol for a sample graded task (10 min); 4) Introduce Attribution Pie visual (10 min); 5) Students complete protocol and Attribution Pie for a returned assignment with teacher conferencing (15-20 min); 6) Connect reflection to forethought by setting process goal for next task (10 min); 7) Biweekly review of cumulative reflection data and attribution pattern tracking (10 min)
resourcesNeeded: Reflection Protocol form; Attribution Pie template; SRL journal; sample graded tasks; attribution pattern tracking sheet; teacher conferencing guide
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Superficial responses: Provide sentence starters and minimum response length with model examples; 2) Upset/avoidant after poor performance: Start with successes, use strength-growth-strength sandwich approach; 3) Conferencing time-prohibitive: Use trained peer reflection partners, rotate teacher conferencing
caseStudySummary: Tyrell, Grade 8, ADHD and ODD, at-risk for dropout. Strategy-based attributions increased from 8% to 62% after 10 weeks. Task attempt rate rose from 30% to 75%. Math grade improved from D- (62%) to C+ (78%).
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Full Reflection Protocol completed for 80% of tasks within 8 weeks (weekly form review); 2) Strategy-based attributions reach 50%+ of all attributions by week 10 (Attribution Pie analysis); 3) Self-evaluation accuracy within 10% of teacher score for 70% of tasks (student vs. teacher score comparison)

bookTitle: Self-Regulated Learning Interventions with At-Risk Youth
author: Timothy J. Cleary
functionalNeedAreas: Help-Seeking Skills, Self-Advocacy, Metacognitive Monitoring, Interpersonal Communication, Independence
strategyName: Cyclical Feedback Loop and Adaptive Help-Seeking Training
briefOverview: Addresses maladaptive help-seeking patterns (avoidance or excessive answer-seeking) by teaching students the Try-Check-Ask protocol and instrumental help-seeking using structured request cards. Builds cyclical feedback skills within the SRL framework.
evidenceBaseSummary: Newman's research showing adaptive help-seeking correlates positively with achievement; studies with at-risk adolescents showing 40-60% increase in instrumental help-seeking; Zimmerman's cyclical feedback model demonstrating self-regulated learners use feedback adaptively.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess baseline help-seeking patterns via questionnaire and observation (15 min); 2) Teach three types of help-seeking with role-plays (15 min); 3) Introduce Try-Check-Ask protocol (10 min); 4) Provide Help Request Cards with instrumental question stems (5 min); 5) Model full Try-Check-Ask cycle with think-aloud (10 min); 6) Guided practice on challenging task with teacher reinforcement (20 min); 7) Weekly log of help-seeking behavior with biweekly goal review (5 min/week, 10 min biweekly)
resourcesNeeded: Help-seeking assessment questionnaire; three types visual poster; Try-Check-Ask protocol card; Help Request Cards; weekly help-seeking log; appropriately challenging practice tasks; teacher observation form
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) ASD/anxiety makes help-seeking aversive: Provide non-verbal options (written cards, digital requests) and low-stakes practice; 2) Reversion to executive help-seeking under pressure: Allow extra time, visual cues, specific praise; 3) Non-supportive classroom culture: Establish norms around help-seeking as strength, integrate into cooperative learning
caseStudySummary: Maya, Grade 6, EFD and Social Anxiety Disorder, at-risk. Instrumental help requests increased from 0 to 4.3/week after 8 weeks. Assignment completion rose from 55% to 88%. SCARED social anxiety subscale decreased from 18 to 11.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) At least 3 instrumental help requests/week within 6 weeks (weekly log and teacher observation); 2) Instrumental-to-executive help ratio reaches 3:1 by week 8 (log categorization); 3) Assignment completion increases 25+ percentage points (gradebook data)

bookTitle: Self-Regulated Learning Interventions with At-Risk Youth
author: Timothy J. Cleary
functionalNeedAreas: Environmental Awareness, Distraction Management, Proactive Planning, Self-Awareness, Coping Strategies
strategyName: Environmental Structuring and Proactive Coping Plans
briefOverview: Teaches students to proactively modify their physical and social environments to support task engagement and reduce barriers. Combines environment engineering with If-Then proactive coping plans to anticipate and preempt self-regulation challenges.
evidenceBaseSummary: Environmental structuring identified by Zimmerman as key SRL strategy; Aspinwall and Taylor proactive coping research showing 30-50% obstacle impact reduction; Gollwitzer implementation intention research showing 2-3x increase in goal-directed behavior likelihood.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Conduct Environment Audit evaluating typical study settings (15 min); 2) Teach environment engineering concept with professional examples (10 min); 3) Create Ideal Environment Checklist with 5-7 features (10 min); 4) Introduce If-Then proactive coping format with modeled examples (10 min); 5) Generate 3-5 personalized If-Then plans (15 min); 6) Implement checklist and plans during monitored work session (20 min); 7) Track daily use for 2 weeks with biweekly effectiveness review (5 min/day, 15 min biweekly)
resourcesNeeded: Environment Audit template; Ideal Environment Checklist; If-Then planning cards; daily tracking log; professional examples; visual supports; optional: headphones, desk organizers, visual timers
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Limited environment control: Focus on controllable micro-environments with portable solutions; 2) If-Then plans forgotten: Mental rehearsal, role-play, visual reminders, start with one plan; 3) Limited generalization: Practice in multiple settings, involve parents/caregivers, use portable checklist
caseStudySummary: Carlos, Grade 11, ADHD and EFD, at-risk for course failure. Implemented 5/7 environmental modifications consistently. If-Then plan adherence at 80% (parent report). Homework completion rose from 35% to 78%. GPA improved from 1.8 to 2.5 over one quarter.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) 5/7+ checklist items implemented in 80% of sessions within 4 weeks (self-report verified by parent/teacher); 2) At least 2 If-Then plans used/week within 4 weeks (self-report and observation); 3) Homework completion increases 30+ percentage points (gradebook data)

author: Michael Wehmeyer
functionalNeedAreas: Goal Setting, Self-Awareness, Problem-Solving, Self-Advocacy, Autonomous Decision-Making
strategyName: Self-Determined Learning Model of Instruction (SDLMI) — Phase 1 Problem-Solving
briefOverview: Wehmeyer's cornerstone model framing learning as problems for students to solve. Phase 1 teaches students to answer four self-directed questions to identify learning needs, assess current knowledge, identify barriers, and generate action steps, shifting locus of control from teacher to student.
evidenceBaseSummary: 30+ published studies including RCTs showing SDLMI improves goal attainment (d=0.40-0.80), self-determination scores, and academic/transition outcomes across disability categories. Validated with LD, ID, ASD, and EBD populations.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Introduce self-determination concept with age-appropriate examples (15 min); 2) Present four Phase 1 Student Questions (10 min); 3) Model answering questions with personally relevant example (15 min); 4) Distribute goal-setting worksheet with Educational Supports explanation (10 min); 5) Students work through Questions 1-2 with peer/teacher support (20 min); 6) Students work through Questions 3-4 identifying barriers and action steps (20 min); 7) Finalize goal statement and action plan, record in portfolio (10 min)
resourcesNeeded: SDLMI Phase 1 Student Question cards; goal-setting worksheet; Educational Supports guide; self-determination portfolio; completed examples; visual supports for modified access; timer
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Goals too broad/unrealistic: Guided questioning to narrow with well-formed examples; 2) Abstract self-assessment difficult for ID students: Concrete visual tools, picture-based scales, portfolio evidence; 3) Resistance to autonomy: Start with constrained choices, gradually expand, validate learning process
caseStudySummary: Keisha, Grade 10, Mild ID and EFD. Independently identified 3 learning goals, attained 2/3 at target level after one semester. ARC Self-Determination Scale score improved from 25th to 52nd percentile. Teacher self-advocacy ratings moved from rarely to frequently.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Completed Phase 1 protocols for 3+ goals within one semester (portfolio review); 2) 60%+ of self-set goals attained at target level (Goal Attainment Scaling); 3) Self-determination score improves 15+ percentile points (pre/post ARC or AIR Scale)

author: Michael Wehmeyer
functionalNeedAreas: Decision-Making, Problem-Solving, Cognitive Flexibility, Self-Advocacy, Independence
briefOverview: Systematic instruction progressing from simple preference-based choices to complex decisions using the DECIDE framework. Engages EF processes of planning, cognitive flexibility, information gathering, consequence prediction, and evaluation.
evidenceBaseSummary: Research showing choice-making increases engagement, reduces problem behavior, and improves performance (Shogren et al., 2004; Lancioni et al., 1996); decision-making instruction improves self-determination and problem-solving scores; adapted for significant cognitive disabilities.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Assess current choice/decision skills on the choice-decision continuum (15 min); 2) Provide structured daily choice opportunities and track satisfaction (embedded, 5 min tracking); 3) Teach DECIDE framework: Define, Explore, Consider, Identify values, Decide, Evaluate (15 min); 4) Model DECIDE with relevant scenario and visual organizer (15 min); 5) Guided practice with a real student decision (20 min); 6) Independent practice with 2-3 scenarios and peer discussion (20 min); 7) Apply DECIDE to real upcoming decision with follow-up evaluation (10 min setup, 10 min follow-up)
resourcesNeeded: Choice/decision assessment tool; DECIDE visual poster and graphic organizer; scenario cards; decision tracking log; choice board templates; visual consequence maps
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Abstract consequence prediction difficult for ID: Use concrete picture-based consequence maps with familiar outcomes; 2) Impulsive decisions without framework: Mandatory pause step with physical thinking cue card; 3) Decision paralysis: Limit initial options to 2-3, teach good-enough principle
caseStudySummary: Jordan, Grade 7, ASD and EFD. Independent DECIDE framework use rose from 0% to 70% of novel decisions after 12 weeks. Novel option selection increased from 5% to 45%. ARC decision-making subscale improved 28%.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Independent DECIDE use for 3/5+ scenarios within 10 weeks (graphic organizer documents); 2) 70%+ of choices aligned with stated goals/values (choice tracking log); 3) Decision-making subscale improves 20%+ (pre/post ARC subscale)

author: Michael Wehmeyer
functionalNeedAreas: Self-Awareness, Self-Advocacy, Communication, IEP Participation, Accommodation Awareness
briefOverview: Systematically builds self-awareness of strengths, limitations, learning preferences, and disability characteristics, paired with self-advocacy skills for communicating needs to teachers, IEP teams, employers, and others. Trains students to lead or co-lead IEP meetings.
evidenceBaseSummary: Test et al. (2005) review identifying self-advocacy as key predictor of post-school success; Martin et al. (2006) and Van Reusen and Bos (1994) showing trained students increase IEP meeting participation, goal setting, and educational ownership.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Conduct self-awareness inventory using Me Map or profile template (20 min); 2) Teach about specific disability and accommodation connections (15 min); 3) Review IEP together ensuring student understanding in own words (15-20 min); 4) Teach I-PLAN advocacy framework (15 min); 5) Role-play self-advocacy scenarios with feedback (20 min); 6) Prepare Self-Advocacy Statement for IEP meeting (20 min); 7) Support student-led IEP meeting portion and debrief (IEP time + 15 min)
resourcesNeeded: Self-awareness inventory/Me Map template; disability information sheets; student-friendly IEP summary; I-PLAN visual and practice cards; role-play scenario cards; Self-Advocacy Statement template; IEP preparation checklist; video examples
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Embarrassment about disability: Normalize as identity aspect, strength-based framing, peer mentors; 2) Overwhelmed by formal IEP environment: Start with informal practice, increase formality gradually, provide scripts/cue cards; 3) Team dominates despite student preparation: Brief team in advance, designate student share time, use visual agenda
caseStudySummary: Priya, Grade 9, SLD-Reading/Writing and EFD. Accurately described learning profile and 4/5 accommodations. Led IEP strengths/goals section for 6 minutes (from 0). Accommodation requests increased from 0.2 to 3.5/week. Reading grades improved from C- to B-.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) Accurately identifies 3+ strengths, 3+ challenges, 3+ accommodations within 6 weeks (inventory accuracy vs. IEP data); 2) 3+ independent accommodation requests/week within 8 weeks (teacher-reported log); 3) Active IEP participation of 3+ minutes addressing 2+ agenda items (meeting observation notes)

author: Michael Wehmeyer
functionalNeedAreas: Self-Regulation, Task Completion, Independence, Behavioral Self-Management, Working Memory Support
briefOverview: Teaches students internalized self-talk to guide behavior through challenging multi-step tasks, progressing through Meichenbaum's sequence from cognitive modeling to overt, faded, and covert self-instruction. Self-instruction script serves as external working memory support gradually internalized.
evidenceBaseSummary: Grounded in Vygotsky's internalization theory and Meichenbaum's cognitive-behavioral modification; meta-analyses showing d=0.50-0.90 for task performance, self-regulation, and academic outcomes across LD, ADHD, and ID; Agran et al. research on task completion and independence.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Identify target multi-step task student struggles with (10 min); 2) Co-create self-instruction script with four statement types: problem definition, planning, self-monitoring, self-reinforcement (15 min); 3) Cognitive Modeling: teacher performs task with script aloud (10 min); 4) Overt External Guidance: student performs while teacher provides prompts (10-15 min); 5) Overt Self-Guidance: student performs while saying script aloud (10-15 min); 6) Faded Self-Guidance: whisper, then lip movements only (10-15 min); 7) Covert Self-Instruction: internal self-talk with accuracy assessment (10 min/session, 3-5 sessions)
resourcesNeeded: Self-instruction script template; task analysis; visual cue cards; task completion checklist; session tracking form; optional audio recorder
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Adolescent self-consciousness about talking aloud: Normalize with professional examples, private practice space, expedite fading; 2) Rote memorization without adaptation: Teach script adaptation for 3+ different tasks; 3) Premature fading causes accuracy loss: Return to previous stage, maintain 3 successful sessions minimum per stage
caseStudySummary: Elijah, Grade 6, Mild ID and ADHD. Independent multi-step task completion rose from 30% to 85% accuracy after 8 weeks. Progressed to covert self-instruction for math, faded for science. Adult prompting decreased from 12 to 2 prompts per task.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) 80%+ task accuracy with covert self-instruction within 8 weeks (teacher-scored checklist); 2) Adult prompting decreases 75%+ from baseline (prompt frequency tracking); 3) Successful self-instruction scripts for 2+ novel tasks demonstrating generalization (observation and task data)

author: Michael Wehmeyer
functionalNeedAreas: Transition Planning, Goal Management, Future Orientation, Self-Evaluation, Independence Skills
strategyName: Goal Setting, Attainment, and Self-Evaluation for Transition Planning
briefOverview: Comprehensive goal management cycle applied to transition domains (post-secondary education, employment, independent living). Students set SMART transition goals based on interests and strengths, create backward-planned action plans, monitor progress, and evaluate outcomes using Goal Attainment Scaling.
evidenceBaseSummary: Students actively participating in transition planning show significantly better post-school outcomes; Whose Future Is It Anyway? curriculum evaluated in multiple studies showing improved self-determination, transition knowledge, and goal attainment; NLTS-2 data supporting self-determination-outcome link.
applicableDiagnoses: Executive Function Disorder; ADHD; Learning Disabilities; Autism Spectrum Disorder; Intellectual Disability; Emotional/Behavioral Disorders
implementationStepsSummary: 1) Administer transition assessments across three domains (30 min); 2) Teach SMART goals applied to transition with model conversions (15 min); 3) Student selects and writes one SMART goal per domain (20 min); 4) Create backward-planned action plan with timeline for each goal (20 min); 5) Introduce Goal Attainment Scaling with 5 outcome levels (15 min); 6) Weekly 10-min progress check-ins with troubleshooting (10 min/week ongoing); 7) End-of-goal self-evaluation using GAS, reflection, and next-cycle goal setting (20 min)
resourcesNeeded: Transition assessment tools; SMART goal template; backward action plan template; GAS form; weekly check-in form; self-evaluation template; transition resource directory; student transition portfolio
challengesSolutionsSummary: 1) Limited post-secondary exposure: Provide structured exploration (job shadows, college visits, virtual tours, guest speakers) before goal-setting; 2) Motivation loss on long-term goals: Break into 2-week sprints with mini-celebrations and visual progress tracking; 3) Abstract future planning difficult for significant cognitive disabilities: Picture-based tools, near-term concrete goals, experiential learning
caseStudySummary: Dante, Grade 11, EFD, ADHD, and SLD. Set and pursued 3 transition goals: completed job shadow (expected level), researched 5 post-secondary programs (more than expected), managed weekly digital calendar (expected level). Self-determination improved 22 percentile points. Self-initiated second job shadow.
successCriteriaSummary: 1) At least one SMART goal per domain with complete action plan within 4 weeks (portfolio review); 2) Expected level or higher on GAS for 2/3 goals by semester end (GAS evaluation); 3) Self-evaluation GAS rating agrees within one level of teacher rating for all goals (student vs. teacher GAS comparison)
